1. **Tesla Lowers Minimum Robotaxi Rider Age:** Tesla has lowered the minimum age for riding in its robotaxi from 13 to 8 years old. An adult or guardian is still required to accompany the child. The host also notes that on X (formerly Twitter), Tesla vehicles are increasingly being spotted operating with no passengers — fully driverless.

2. **Robotaxi Infrastructure Questions:** A viewer question raises the issue of cleaning, charging, and maintenance infrastructure for robotaxi fleets not yet being fully deployed. The host speculates that service centers could double as overnight cleaning and maintenance hubs, and expresses hope that fleet managers can get early access to the service as they would represent a significant early success story.

## Humanoid Bots

1. **First Markets for Optimus Disruption:** When asked which markets Optimus will disrupt first, the host identifies factories, logistics, and warehouses — all behind-the-scenes industrial settings — as the most likely early deployment sectors.

## Market & Competition

1. **Chinese Solar Panel Tariffs and US Competitiveness:** Chinese solar panels face a 100% import tariff in the US. The host argues that US-made panels only need to be priced within approximately 40–50% of Chinese production costs to remain competitive after tariffs, and notes proximity to US deployment sites as an additional logistics advantage.

2. **GLJ Research Criticized:** The host criticizes GLJ Research's Gordon Johnson harshly, arguing his record on Tesla calls has been "impressively bad" and questioning the undisclosed funding behind his bearish research. The host suggests the SEC should investigate.

Notable quotes and data:
- Delivery consensus for Q1 2026: **365,000 units** (described as "kind of low")
- Australian petrol price: **AU$2.54/liter (~US$9.61/gallon equivalent)**
- Australian EV search spike: **76%**; year-on-year EV sales jump: **95%**
- US solar capacity: **267 gigawatts**, supplying ~**8% of US electricity demand** (vs. 1% in 2015)
- Lucid potential shareholder dilution from Saudi fund: **65–70%** depending on strike price
- Additional Saudi investment fund pledge to Lucid: **$4 billion**
- Lucid consecutive quarters of record deliveries: **8**
- Tesla folding supercharger units save ~**20% on cost** and cut installation time **in half**
- Battery imbalance scenario: **350 MWh** system reduced to ~**75% effective capacity**
- Model 3 European import tariff (from US): approximately **14–15%**
- Canadian EV subsidy price ceiling: **CA$35,000 (~US$28,000)**
- Tesla robotaxi minimum rider age: lowered from **13 to 8** (adult still required)

articles.title: Why Tesla And Nvidia Stock are Setting Up to RALLY
articles.body:
Jo Bhakdi analyzes the broader market rally on what he describes as the best NASDAQ day of the year (up 3.24%), driven by signals that the Iran conflict may be de-escalating and Fed commentary suggesting potential rate cuts. The core thesis is that Tesla and Nvidia are "coiled springs" set up for a significant rally, with Tesla specifically positioned for a major Q2 2026 inflection point tied to FSD v14.3 and Cybercab production ramping. However, he cautions that a 25% tail risk of further Iran escalation justifies remaining underexposed and avoiding leverage.

## Autonomous Driving
1. **FSD v14.3 as the "Final Unlock" of Full Autonomy:** Jo Bhakdi states that FSD version 14.3 represents what he believes will be the final unlock of full autonomy, and he expects it to arrive within the next 14 days at minimum, or within the next month. He describes this as a critical catalyst for Tesla's stock repricing, arguing that Tesla has already prepared multiple cities for autonomous deployment and that the combination of this software release with Cybercab production ramping could mark a genuine, large-scale rollout of autonomy.

2. **Q2 2026 as the Autonomy Inflection Point:** Bhakdi assigns a 75% or higher probability that Q2 2026 is the moment Tesla rolls out autonomy "for real." He frames this as a potential "absolute gamechanger" that could initiate what he calls "the great repricing of the stock," contrasting it with Q1 2026 which he says played out exactly as he predicted — a period of deflation with no catalysts.

## Robotaxi
1. **Cybercab Production Ramp as a Key Catalyst:** Bhakdi highlights that Cybercab production is ramping up and, combined with FSD v14.3 and pre-prepared city deployments, could represent the convergence of catalysts needed to trigger Tesla's revaluation. He refers to this combination as a potentially historic moment for the company.

## Market & Competition
1. **NASDAQ Year-to-Date Performance and Market Context:** The NASDAQ is down 7.64% year-to-date as of the video, making the single-day 3.24% gain the best day of the year. Bhakdi characterizes the prior months as an "extremely exhausting grind" and a "pretty much a bloodbath," driven by Iran conflict uncertainty, oil price disruption via the Strait of Hormuz blockade, and broader macro fears.

2. **Nvidia's Valuation as a Market Signal:** Bhakdi notes that Nvidia currently has its lowest price-to-earnings ratio in 7 years, despite being up 4.66% on the day. He states Nvidia has dropped below a $4 trillion market cap and is also down roughly 7% year-to-date. He argues this extreme compression in Nvidia's valuation, alongside dramatic margin growth and AI positioning, illustrates how much of a "coiled spring" the broader AI market represents. He predicts Nvidia will rally back to $190 "at the millisecond" the Iran situation resolves.

3. **Tesla Down 18% Year-to-Date:** Tesla is down approximately 18% year-to-date, underperforming even Bhakdi's own Pioneer Alpha portfolio (which he says is down roughly 10-11%). He frames this underperformance as expected given the lack of catalysts in Q1, and argues it makes the Q2 setup even more compelling.

4. **Geopolitical Tail Risk Framing:** Bhakdi estimates a 75% probability the market has bottomed and a 25% tail risk of further escalation in Iran. He warns that if the U.S. deploys boots on the ground, it would be a "complete disaster," potentially sending oil to $150–$200 and devastating markets through at least 2027. He views this low-probability but high-impact scenario as the primary reason to avoid leverage despite the attractive setup.

## Financials
1. **Portfolio Positioning — Underexposed but Fading Back In:** Bhakdi discloses that he has significantly reduced his exposure across Tesla, Nvidia, and MicroStrategy during the recent volatility, while maintaining Tesla as a core position he will not sell. He describes himself as currently under 100% exposure and says he is cautiously beginning to fade back in, but is unwilling to take on leverage given the remaining Iran tail risk.

Notable quotes and data:
- NASDAQ up 3.24% on the day — described as "by far the best day in the year"
- NASDAQ down 7.64% year-to-date
- Tesla down ~18% year-to-date
- Pioneer Alpha portfolio down ~10–11% year-to-date
- Tesla up ~4% on the day; Nvidia up 4.66% on the day
- Nvidia below $4 trillion market cap; also down ~7% year-to-date
- Nvidia at its lowest P/E ratio in 7 years
- FSD v14.3 expected within "the next 14 days" minimum, "next month" at the latest
- 75% probability assigned to Q2 2026 being the real autonomy rollout moment
- 75% probability the market has currently bottomed; 25% tail risk of further escalation
- Tail risk scenario: oil goes to "$150–$200" if Iran conflict escalates
- Bhakdi: "I am willing to bet that Q2 is very likely 75% or higher probability the moment in time when Tesla actually rolls out autonomy for real, no joke."
- Bhakdi on Nvidia: "That will happen at the millisecond where this Iran disaster is over."

articles.title: Tesla FSD Going Global Every Country Explained
articles.body:
This video provides a comprehensive global status report on Tesla's FSD rollout, arguing that the technology has crossed a critical inflection point from "promise" to "product." The host walks through every country where FSD is live, pending, or in trials, while highlighting a viral pedestrian-prediction clip as evidence of a technical breakthrough in AI-based intent modeling. The core thesis is that the market is still pricing FSD as a vehicle feature rather than a global software platform, and that April 2026 represents a convergence of catalysts — regulatory, technical, and commercial — that could trigger a significant re-rating.

## Autonomous Driving

1. **FSD Supervised live in seven countries:** FSD Supervised is currently approved and available to customers in the United States (live since 2023, with unsupervised FSD now active in geofenced areas of Austin), Canada, Mexico, Puerto Rico, Australia, New Zealand, and South Korea (launched November 2025). China has partial approval but remains constrained by data localization laws that prevent Tesla from transferring training videos outside the country, and the government has banned the term "Full Self-Driving," requiring Tesla to rename it "intelligent assisted driving." Elon Musk has said he expects full approval in China in 2026.

2. **South Korea's rapid adoption and performance:** South Korea launched FSD Supervised in November 2025 and has already logged 5 million FSD miles in just 100 days. The Korea Expressway Corporation rated highway performance as "excellent," making it one of the fastest-ramping international deployments on record.

3. **Abu Dhabi trials mark Middle East debut:** Supervised FSD trials on public roads launched in Abu Dhabi on March 1st under the Abu Dhabi Mobility Authority, representing the first confirmed FSD footprint in the Middle East. These are structured trials, not yet a full consumer launch.

4. **Netherlands expected to be first EU approval on April 10th:** The Dutch Vehicle Authority (RDW) has been working with Tesla on an 18-month testing program. Tesla completed 1.66 million km of testing on European roads and conducted over 13,000 customer ride-alongs. All documentation for UN Regulation 171 approval and Article 39 exemptions has been submitted. The RDW confirmed in late March that they are in the final internal review stage, with an expected decision date of April 10th. The host is careful to note this is an expected date, not a confirmed approval.

5. **Netherlands approval could cascade to 27 EU countries:** Under EU Regulation 2018/858, once the Netherlands grants type approval, other EU member states can recognize that approval through a mutual recognition process, potentially opening the door to all 27 EU countries without each running a full independent review. Tesla anticipates a coordinated EU-wide rollout by summer, covering Germany, France, Italy, Belgium, Austria, and the Nordic countries. Tesla has been running FSD demonstration ride-alongs across Germany, France, Italy, Denmark, and Switzerland through March 31st, extending the program multiple times due to popularity.

6. **Israel and Japan are next in the pipeline:** Israel's Transport Minister publicly stated FSD is "coming soon," following government approval of supervised autonomous driving trials on public roads in February. In Japan, Tesla's Japan President Hiroshi Hashimoto confirmed FSD is targeting a launch this year, with active testing underway in Tokyo using both Model Y and Model 3 vehicles. If approved, an over-the-air update could instantly activate FSD for approximately 40,000 Teslas already on Japanese roads.

7. **FSD version 14.3 described as "the last big piece of the puzzle":** Elon Musk confirmed on March 19th that FSD version 14.3 is in testing, with a wide release expected in early to mid-April 2026. Elon described it as adding an order-of-magnitude larger neural network with more reasoning capabilities and reinforcement learning. He described the expected result as making the car feel "almost like a sentient being." This version is characterized as completing the core architecture rather than being an incremental update.

8. **Pedestrian intent prediction clip signals architectural breakthrough:** A viral clip published through Tesla's official account showed the FSD system slowing and yielding for a pedestrian who was standing on the sidewalk — not yet moving toward the road — and then the pedestrian stepped off the curb seconds later, exactly as anticipated. The host explains this is made possible by FSD version 14's end-to-end neural network architecture, where perception, prediction, and planning are unified rather than separate modules. The system reads body language cues — orientation, head direction, weight shift — to model likely intent rather than simply reacting to detected movement.

9. **Safety data trending toward 10x human safety:** Tesla's Q2 2025 safety report showed FSD Supervised vehicles were involved in approximately one crash every 7.6 million miles driven, compared to the national average of roughly one crash every 670,000 miles for human drivers — placing current FSD at roughly seven times safer on a per-mile basis. Elon posted on X that Tesla AI self-driving "will be" greater than 10 times safer than human driving, which the host clarifies is a forward-looking statement, not a current confirmed statistic. The host acknowledges legitimate methodological debates: Tesla's numbers are self-reported, not independently verified, and FSD may operate more frequently in favorable conditions. Tesla's total accumulated training data stands at 8.4 billion miles.

10. **Narrative shift among skeptics and media:** Motor Trend named FSD Supervised the winner of their best driver assistance system award for 2026, with editors explicitly acknowledging their previous skepticism and writing that "the majority of the flaws we noted in earlier versions have largely been addressed." At CES 2026, industry analysts noted that the entire autonomous driving industry has converged on Tesla-like vision-based solutions, with one analyst calling it "an admission that alternative paths were dead ends." Germany's biggest car magazine tested FSD Supervised and called it "genuinely stunning." NHTSA is noted as actively investigating FSD's performance.

## Robotaxi

1. **Austin robotaxi service accumulating incident data:** The Austin robotaxi service — currently operating in geofenced areas with unsupervised FSD — has had roughly 14 reported incidents over 18 months. The host presents this alongside the broader limitations disclosure, noting that fully unsupervised FSD remains limited to small geofenced boundaries in just one city.

2. **Cybercab production begins in April:** Cybercab production is noted as beginning in April 2026, coinciding with the broader cluster of FSD catalysts. Tesla is also hiring AI safety operators in 18 US cities across 12 states, which the host reads as preparation for expanding the unsupervised robotaxi footprint beyond Austin.

3. **Geofence expansion is the key signal to watch:** The host identifies the expansion of the unsupervised FSD zone beyond Austin as the critical indicator that a nationwide robotaxi rollout is accelerating. When new cities are added to the unsupervised zone, that signals the inflection from pilot to platform.

## Market & Competition

1. **Waymo constrained; Tesla's global reach is unmatched:** The host notes that Waymo operates in only a handful of US cities and that Cruise is effectively paused, while Tesla is pursuing simultaneous global expansion across seven approved markets and multiple pending ones. No other autonomous driving system has the same fleet reach for an over-the-air activation model.

2. **Industry convergence on Tesla's camera-only approach:** At CES 2026, the broader autonomous driving industry was noted to have converged on Tesla-like vision-based solutions, validating Tesla's long-contested camera-only architecture after years of competitors mocking the approach.

## Financials

1. **FSD as a global subscription platform — the unpriced opportunity:** The host argues the market is pricing FSD as a vehicle feature rather than a global software platform. At $99 to $199 per month per subscriber across a fleet growing toward 10 million vehicles, a full global FSD subscription rollout could generate a software revenue stream the host compares to entire SaaS companies. Q1 2026 earnings in late April are flagged as the next financial data point to watch, specifically FSD take rate, subscription revenue, and forward guidance on unsupervised expansion.

Notable quotes and data:
- Elon Musk posted that "Tesla AI self-driving will be greater than 10 times safer than human driving" (forward-looking, not a current confirmed statistic)
- FSD Supervised: ~1 crash per 7.6 million miles (Tesla Q2 2025 safety report, self-reported)
- Human driver national average: ~1 crash per 670,000 miles
- Implied current safety advantage: roughly 7x on a per-mile basis
- Tesla accumulated training data: 8.4 billion miles
- South Korea: 5 million FSD miles in 100 days since November 2025 launch
- Netherlands testing: 1.66 million km on European roads, 13,000+ customer ride-alongs
- FSD currently live in 7 countries; unsupervised active in geofenced Austin only
- Austin robotaxi: ~14 reported incidents in 18 months
- FSD version 14.3: wide release expected early-to-mid April 2026
- Cybercab production begins April 2026
- Tesla hiring AI safety operators in 18 US cities across 12 states
- FSD subscription pricing range cited: $99–$199/month
- Japan: ~40,000 Teslas already on roads that could receive FSD via OTA if approved
- Motor Trend: FSD Supervised named best driver assistance system for 2026
- Netherlands expected decision date: April 10, 2026
- EU mutual recognition regulation: 2018/858
- Dutch regulatory framework: UN Regulation 171, Article 39 exemptions

articles.title: Tesla Might Be Building Something Completely New
articles.body:
This video, published April 1, 2026, features host Herbert and guest Brian White (of the YouTube channel Futuraza) discussing Tesla's potential upcoming vehicles, the expanding Cybercab testing footprint across the U.S., the growth of Tesla's unsupervised robotaxi geofence in Austin, and the broader vision of Tesla as a full transportation ecosystem. The core argument is that Tesla is methodically building toward autonomous transportation across multiple vehicle form factors and infrastructure layers, from Cybercabs and Model Ys to the Vegas Loop.

## Electric Vehicles

1. **New vehicle teased by Elon Musk:** Elon Musk recently posted that "something way cooler than a minivan is coming," prompting speculation about what Tesla's next vehicle might be. An old Tesla lineup image previously shared by the company showed silhouettes of vehicles that appeared to be placeholders, with some interpreted as a Cybercab, a human-drivable version (potentially dubbed the NV91), and possibly an unknown third vehicle — though Brian White notes the two end silhouettes appear identical, suggesting they were just placeholders and the Roadster was likely omitted entirely from that image.

2. **Cyber SUV speculation:** Images from what appears to be a Tesla design studio in France show a render or sketch of a Cyber SUV-style vehicle in the background, fueling speculation. Brian White finds the Cyber SUV visually appealing — he says it looks better than the Cybertruck — and notes it could potentially use a plastic-impregnated steel material similar to the Cybercab rather than stainless steel, reducing weight and avoiding the need for a new paint shop. However, he is skeptical it will happen soon, citing the significant engineering overhead required (including a different rear casting to allow a third row) and Tesla's stated focus on autonomy over new human-driven vehicles.

3. **Cybertruck floor space and possible Cyber SUV incentive:** Herbert references a Giga Texas factory floor map discussed in a prior interview with Joe Tegtmeyer, which showed that the floor space dedicated to Cybertruck production is massive relative to current demand, while Cybercab has a smaller footprint. Brian White finds this the most compelling argument for a Cyber SUV — that Tesla may need to better utilize existing Cybertruck production equipment rather than let it sit underused.

4. **Model Y L as the most likely near-term new product:** Brian White expresses high confidence that the Model Y L (a longer version of the Model Y) is the most likely upcoming new vehicle for the U.S. market. He cites the observation of what appeared to be a Model Y body-in-white arriving in an inbound crate at Giga Texas — noting that outbound vehicles would be sealed inside before shipment, making this likely an inbound validation unit from either Giga Berlin or Giga Shanghai. He draws a parallel to how Tesla previously shipped Model 3 and Model Y bodies-in-white from Fremont to Shanghai during production validation. He also notes that scheduled downtime at Giga Texas is coming up in the next week or two, which he speculates could be related to line changes. A Model Y XL (three-row stretched version) is also discussed but considered far less confirmed.

5. **Robovan not on near-term roadmap:** Brian White states that when he asked Tesla engineers about the Robovan, they said they were "not even thinking about it," and that either Franz von Holzhausen or Elon Musk confirmed it is not yet being actively pursued — describing it as something they are excited about in concept but "not time" for yet. Elon's comment that the upcoming vehicle is "something much cooler than a minivan" is interpreted as ruling out the Robovan as the teased product.

## Robotaxi

1. **Cybercab testing footprint — 36 units spotted across 7 regions:** According to the Cybercab tracker, 36 Cybercabs have been spotted across the country, across seven testing regions: Austin, Bay Area, Chicago, Alaska, Boston, Buffalo, and Washington D.C. Twenty-seven of those are in Austin, likely because they are being built at Giga Texas and Austin is the primary robotaxi testing market. Additional sightings in locations like Oklahoma and Los Angeles suggest the true count is higher than tracked. Joe Tegtmeyer has also observed Cybercabs disappearing from the outbound lot at Giga Texas within a day or two of being spotted, indicating they are actively being deployed.

2. **Cybercab crash testing underway:** Joe Tegtmeyer captured footage showing 12 Cybercabs at a crash testing facility, with several showing signs of completed or in-progress tests. Eight additional Cybercabs were spotted at the Alpine lot on the same day. The video also briefly highlights a unique aerial-style view of a Cybercab's front hood area and door from above.

3. **Model Y validation rigs spotted across multiple cities:** In addition to Cybercabs, Model Ys with validation-mounted sensor rigs are being spotted in multiple cities simultaneously, including Garland, Texas (near Dallas-Fort Worth). Brian White clarifies that these vehicles are not doing HD mapping from scratch like competitors — Tesla already has the maps it needs and is using LiDAR-equipped validation vehicles to confirm the accuracy of existing map data. He argues that Tesla will likely be able to cross-reference data sets efficiently enough that not every street will need a dedicated validation drive.

4. **Unsupervised geofence in Austin expanded:** The unsupervised Tesla robotaxi geofence in Austin has been expanded, with a user reporting that the unsupervised Model Y robotaxi now crosses the river and reaches downtown areas it previously did not serve. Tesla Robotaxi's official account responded to the user's post with "Map found," confirming the expansion. The broader area around Austin still uses safety operators (referred to as "safety hitchhikers"), while only the smaller confirmed geofenced zone is fully unsupervised.

5. **Waymo data on robotaxi trip occupancy:** Herbert references data from Waymo showing that 90% of robotaxi trips have two or fewer passengers — with 71% being single-passenger trips and 5% being zero-passenger (likely repositioning) trips. This is used to defend the Cybercab's two-seat design, with the argument that Model Ys in the fleet can handle the roughly 10% of trips requiring three or more passengers.

6. **Fleet ownership advantage for quality control:** Brian White argues that because Tesla owns the Cybercab fleet (rather than selling directly to consumers), any quality issue or recall is far easier to manage — Tesla can schedule service on its own timeline, run overnight repair sessions, and cycle vehicles through quickly without inconveniencing individual owners.

## Autonomous Driving

1. **Vegas Loop as a stepping stone to full autonomy:** Elon Musk posted that Teslas are currently operating in tunnels under Las Vegas transporting millions of people via the Boring Company's Vegas Loop. The newest station opened at the Fontainebleau Resort. Brian White explains that the Loop is not yet autonomous because Tesla did not want to create a software fork earlier than necessary — maintaining two separate code bases would have been costly. He believes the Loop will transition to autonomous operation within a few months of the wider surface-level robotaxi rollout, at which point a unified software layer can be applied.

2. **FSD reliability in personal use:** Brian White states that it has been approximately one year since he has needed to make any critical intervention while using FSD, across two countries and three states. He describes a recent trip home from a doctor's appointment where FSD completed the entire drive including pulling into the driveway without any input. He contrasts this with his recent experience in China with human drivers, expressing strong preference for autonomous driving.

3. **Robotaxi vs. unsupervised FSD complexity:** Brian White argues that robotaxi is actually easier to solve than full unsupervised FSD for personal vehicles, because the robotaxi fleet has dedicated remote operators at HQ on standby — whereas personal FSD currently relies on the driver as the safety backstop. He notes that when Waymo makes a mistake, the public understands it is the system; when a personal FSD vehicle makes a mistake, it is unclear whether it was the driver or the software.

4. **Cybercab FSD form factor advantage:** Brian White argues that the Cybercab's smaller form factor may actually make it easier to deploy as a self-driving vehicle than larger models like the Cybertruck, which had early FSD examples clipping curbs. A smaller vehicle would have more margin within lane boundaries. He contrasts this with early Cybertruck FSD, where the large footprint created curb-clipping issues.

Notable quotes and data:
- Elon Musk: "Something way cooler than a minivan is coming."
- Elon Musk (on Vegas Loop): "Teslas are in tunnels under Vegas transporting millions of people."
- Tesla Robotaxi official account responded to geofence expansion post with: "Map found."
- Waymo data: 71% of robotaxi trips are single-passenger; 5% are zero-passenger; ~90% have two or fewer passengers; ~10% have three or more.
- 36 Cybercabs spotted total; 27 in Austin across 7 testing regions.
- 12 Cybercabs observed at crash testing facility; 8 at Alpine lot (per Joe Tegtmeyer).
- Brian White: "I'm confident that when they release it, it will perform at least better than Waymo."
- Brian White on the Vegas Loop: "I imagine it'll come within a few months of the wider rollout of robotaxi on the surface."
- Scheduled downtime at Giga Texas reportedly coming in the next week or two (from video publish date of April 1, 2026).

articles.title: Why I am Getting READY for Tesla Stock (and the Market) to EXPLODE UPWARDS
articles.body:
In this April 1, 2026 video, Jo Bhakdi argues that Tesla stock and the broader market are positioned like a "coiled spring" ready for a major rally, contingent on President Trump announcing an end to the ongoing Iran war conflict. He connects the geopolitical situation to a near-term Tesla catalyst — the imminent rollout of FSD 14.3 and the Cybercab launch — framing April 2026 as the long-anticipated inflection point for Tesla's robotaxi thesis.

## Market & Competition
1. **Geopolitical Overhang Creating a Coiled Spring:** Jo Bhakdi describes the current market as a "coiled spring" being held back primarily by the Iran war and rising oil prices tied to the Strait of Hormuz. He notes the NASDAQ is down 6.1% year-to-date and has been down approximately 4% over the last six months, despite what he characterizes as the largest bull market in history driven by AI productivity gains. Tesla specifically is down 16% year-to-date, having fallen from $450 in late January.

2. **Trump Address as a Binary Market Event:** Bhakdi frames a presidential address scheduled for 9:00 p.m. that evening as a binary event with two extreme outcomes. He assigns a 75–80% probability that Trump announces an end to the conflict and declares victory, which he believes would trigger a significant market rally. He assigns a 25% tail risk to Trump doubling down and escalating the conflict, which he says would cause a market crash. He describes himself as approximately one-third risk-on heading into the announcement, with plans to increase exposure significantly if the outcome is favorable.

3. **Tesla Rally Potential to $450:** Bhakdi argues that Tesla could recover all the way back to $450 — its early February level — simply on the resolution of geopolitical uncertainty, without needing any new fundamental catalyst. He notes that even if Tesla moves up 5% before an investor can react, there remains substantial further upside to $420, $430, or $450 before the Cybercab rollout adds additional momentum.

## Autonomous Driving
1. **FSD 14.3 Confirmed for Imminent Release:** Bhakdi highlights that Elon Musk has confirmed FSD 14.3 is coming out the following week. He describes this version as being 10x larger than the previous version, which itself was already considered by some to be close to ready for commercial deployment with minor fine-tuning. FSD 14.3 was already beginning to roll out to early users on the day of the video, with a broader rollout to all users expected the following week.

2. **FSD 14.3 as the Potential Commercial Robotaxi Version:** Bhakdi states that his expectation — shared by a number of other Tesla investors, analysts, and experts he references — is that FSD 14.3 is the version that will serve as the commercial-grade software for robotaxi deployment. He emphasizes this is not confirmed but represents the prevailing thesis among Tesla bulls.

## Robotaxi
1. **Cybercab Manufacturing Ramping in April 2026:** Bhakdi notes that April 2026 coincides with the Cybercab launch and Cybercab manufacturing entering high gear. He frames this as a validation of the timeline he has been publicly maintaining for nearly a year — that April 2026 would be the robotaxi inflection point.

2. **Robotaxi Fleet at 539 Vehicles, 94 in Austin:** Referencing a "robotaxi tracker," Bhakdi reports that Tesla's total robotaxi fleet stands at 539 vehicles, with 94 of those located in Austin. He acknowledges that critics may say his April inflection point prediction is wrong because the fleet has not yet reached his stated threshold, but he maintains his prediction and says he may be off by one to two months at most.

3. **Over 1,000 Robotaxis Targeted for April 2026:** Bhakdi reiterates a specific prediction he has held for nearly a year: that over 1,000 robotaxis would be rolled out in April 2026. He acknowledges the fleet tracker does not yet show that number but stands by the timeline, allowing for a possible one-to-two-month slip to May or June 2026.

Notable quotes and data:
- Tesla year-to-date performance: down 16% as of April 1, 2026
- Tesla peak referenced: $450 in late January / early February 2026
- NASDAQ composite: down 6.1% year-to-date; down ~4% over the last six months
- FSD 14.3 described as "10x larger than the previous version"
- FSD 14.3 confirmed by Elon Musk for release "next week" (week of ~April 7, 2026); early user rollout already began on April 1
- Total Tesla robotaxi fleet per tracker: 539 vehicles; 94 in Austin
- Bhakdi's robotaxi prediction: 1,000+ robotaxis deployed in April 2026
- Probability assigned to Trump ending Iran conflict that evening: 75–80%
- Bhakdi's personal risk positioning heading into the event: approximately one-third of full desired exposure

articles.title: FSD v14.3 Drops This Week. Everything You Need to Know
articles.body:
This video from the "brighterwithherbert" channel covers the imminent wide release of FSD version 14.3, which was in employee beta testing as of the video's publication. The host's core argument is that FSD v14.3 represents the most architecturally significant update since Tesla moved to end-to-end neural networks in version 12, and that it functions as the final major software piece needed to enable unsupervised robotaxi deployment at scale. The video carefully separates confirmed facts from speculation, while acknowledging Elon Musk's history of overselling FSD updates.

## Autonomous Driving

1. **FSD v14.3 enters employee beta, wide release imminent:** Elon Musk announced that FSD version 14.3 entered employee beta testing as of the night before the video was published. Wide release is expected by the end of the week of April 2, 2026, though the host notes it could slip to early the following week. The update targets hardware 4 vehicles first and is described as the largest architectural change to FSD since the move to end-to-end neural networks in version 12.

2. **10x larger neural network — what it actually means:** Version 14.3 features a neural network approximately 10 times larger in terms of parameters than the model currently running on Tesla vehicles. The host explains that more parameters allow the AI to recognize more patterns, handle more edge cases, and make more nuanced decisions — potentially understanding vastly more intersection types, pedestrian behaviors, construction patterns, and weather conditions than the prior model. This is described as a qualitative shift, not just incremental improvement.

3. **Reasoning as the defining new capability:** The central new feature in v14.3 is reasoning — the ability for the car to plan ahead and interpret context rather than just react to immediate stimuli. Concrete examples given include: evaluating multiple parking spots and choosing the best one; navigating construction zones by reading conflicting signals from cones, road markings, and flaggers; and reading the intent of other drivers at ambiguous intersections like unprotected left turns, four-way stops, and roundabouts. Tesla VP of AI Ashok Swami confirmed that some reasoning capabilities had already shipped in v14.2, specifically in navigation route changes during construction and parking option evaluation, with v14.3 representing a full deployment of capabilities built gradually.

4. **Sentience claim — what Elon likely means:** Elon Musk stated in October that "by version 14.3, your car will feel like it's sentient." The host interprets this not as a claim of machine consciousness, but as a description of anticipatory, context-aware driving behavior that mimics human intuition — for example, slowing for a pedestrian based on body language before they step into the road, or smoothly repositioning away from an erratically drifting driver. Tesla posted a video the prior week showing FSD predicting a pedestrian's intent to cross before they stepped off the curb, cited as a preview of this capability.

5. **Reasoning bridges supervised and unsupervised FSD:** The host argues that reasoning is the specific capability that allows Tesla to expand from supervised FSD to unsupervised operation at scale, because it enables the car to handle ambiguous situations independently rather than requiring human intervention. The end-to-end neural network (v12) provided the architecture; versions 12 through 14.2 built reliability; unsupervised FSD was activated for non-employee owners in a geofenced area in Austin on March 18, 2026; and v14.3's reasoning layer is what the host identifies as the capability needed to expand beyond that limited zone.

6. **Hardware 4 vs. Hardware 3 — the divide:** The 10x larger model requires significantly more compute and memory than hardware 3 can provide. Hardware 4 has double the memory of hardware 3 (hardware 4: 16 GB implied; hardware 3: 8 GB stated). The full v14.3 model cannot run at full resolution on hardware 3. Tesla confirmed during their earnings call that an "FSD v14 light" — a distilled or quantized compressed version of the model — is in development for hardware 3 vehicles. Ashok Swami indicated this is expected in Q2 2026, which the host interprets as late June 2026 at the earliest, noting Tesla's track record suggests it could be later. The host expresses uncertainty about whether the reasoning layer will be included in the light version due to its additional compute requirements.

7. **Additional expected features in v14.3:** Beyond reasoning, the host lists several anticipated improvements: (1) "Banish" (reverse summon) — the car drops you at the door and parks itself autonomously, described as technically feasible for the first time with v14.3's reasoning; (2) improved Actually Smart Summon, currently still running on older version 12 and Autopilot stacks; (3) better navigation and route planning including detour handling; (4) improved performance on unmapped and private roads, driveways, and unmarked residential areas; and (5) better handling of degraded conditions such as rain and fog, an area NHTSA is actively investigating.

8. **FSD safety data and fleet mileage cited:** The host references Tesla safety data showing FSD is approximately 7 to 11 times safer than a human driver on a per-mile basis. The Tesla fleet has logged 8.4 billion miles.

9. **The end-to-end neural network replaced 300,000 lines of code:** The host notes that when Tesla shipped version 12 with the end-to-end neural network, it replaced 300,000 lines of handcoded rules with a single AI model trained on real-world data, framing this as the foundational architectural shift that v14.3 now builds upon.

## Robotaxi

1. **Cybercab production begins this month, priced under $30,000:** The host states that Cybercab production begins in April 2026. The vehicle is purpose-built with no steering wheel and no pedals, and is priced under $30,000. It will run FSD version 14.3 from day one, directly linking the software release to the robotaxi hardware launch.

2. **Tesla hiring AI safety operators in 18 cities across 12 states:** Tesla is actively hiring AI safety operators in 18 cities across 12 states, which the host interprets as preparation for a robotaxi rollout rather than a research project. FSD v14.3 is described as the software that enables expansion to these 18 cities.

3. **Unsupervised FSD already live in Austin geofenced area:** Tesla activated unsupervised FSD for non-employee owners in a geofenced area in Austin on March 18, 2026. Version 14.3's reasoning capabilities are framed as what's needed to expand this unsupervised operation beyond the current limited geofenced zone to entire cities and eventually nationwide.

4. **Q1 2026 earnings call flagged as key investor catalyst:** The host advises investors to watch Tesla's Q1 earnings call in late April 2026, citing forward guidance on FSD v14.3 reception, Cybercab integration, and unsupervised expansion plans as the most important data points for the quarter. Early user reception of v14.3 is also flagged as a potential stock-moving catalyst.

## Electric Vehicles

1. **Hardware 3 owners facing frustration over FSD investment:** The host acknowledges that hardware 3 vehicle owners who paid $12,000 for FSD are facing a meaningful capability gap, as v14.3 will not be available to them at launch. FSD v14 light is the planned solution, expected no earlier than late June 2026, and likely without the full reasoning layer. The host frames this as a real tension created by AI outpacing the older chip's capabilities.

Notable quotes and data:
- Elon Musk (October): "By version 14.3, your car will feel like it's sentient."
- Elon Musk called v14.3 "the last big piece of the puzzle."
- FSD v14.3 neural network is 10x larger in parameters than the current model.
- Hardware 3 has 8 GB of memory; hardware 4 has double that (16 GB implied).
- Tesla FSD safety data: approximately 7 to 11 times safer than a human driver per mile.
- Tesla fleet total mileage: 8.4 billion miles logged.
- End-to-end neural network in v12 replaced 300,000 lines of handcoded rules.
- Cybercab: purpose-built, no steering wheel, no pedals, under $30,000, production starting April 2026.
- Tesla hiring AI safety operators in 18 cities across 12 states.
- Unsupervised FSD activated for non-employee owners in Austin geofenced area on March 18, 2026.
- FSD v14 light for hardware 3 expected Q2 2026, host estimates late June at earliest.
- FSD paid by some hardware 3 owners: $12,000.
- Q1 2026 Tesla earnings call expected in late April 2026.

articles.title: This Is The Tesla Debate Everyone Is Watching
articles.body:
This video from brighterwithherbert features a debate-style discussion between the host and guest Jo Bhakdi — economist, investor, and tech entrepreneur — on whether Tesla's stock is headed toward a $10 trillion valuation or a 65% crash. The video frames the bull vs. bear argument as fundamentally a question of timeline: whether Tesla can commercially deploy unsupervised robotaxi service in 2026, or whether delays will allow competitors to close the gap. The core thesis is that Tesla's car business faces real pressure, but the bears are missing the transformative potential of robotaxi, Optimus, and energy infrastructure.

## Robotaxi
1. **Cathie Wood's Bull Case — Winner-Take-Most Robotaxi Market:** Cathie Wood of ARK Invest is presented as the most bullish Tesla voice, arguing the autonomous taxi market will grow from roughly $1 billion today to a $10 trillion opportunity within 5 to 10 years. She frames it as a winner-take-most market — similar to Uber vs. Lyft — driven by network effects, with Tesla winning on safety, speed, and cost. She specifically argues this is not just about replacing ride-hailing but ultimately replacing personal transportation altogether when per-mile costs drop below a dollar.

2. **Jo Bhakdi's Timeline Framework — 2026 or Risk Losing the Edge:** Jo Bhakdi argues the entire bull case hinges on Tesla solving commercial robotaxi deployment this year (2026). If Tesla achieves unsupervised, commercial-scale FSD this year, it wins decisively because no competitor — including Waymo — can match its manufacturing scale for approximately 12 to 18 months. In that window, Tesla could potentially deploy 500,000 to a million vehicles, triggering irreversible network effects. However, he warns that every quarter of delay shrinks this advantage, as legacy automakers like Volkswagen and Toyota begin taking autonomy more seriously, aided by Nvidia's partnerships across the industry.

3. **The Two-Year Outer Limit:** Bhakdi sets 2028 as the outer bound for Tesla to still achieve winner-take-most status. He argues that even if Tesla is slow to scale, by approximately 2029, a competitor entering the market cannot overcome Tesla's network density, brand, and deployment scale — network effects lock in Tesla's lead at that point. But beyond a two-year delay from now, he sees the competitive risk as growing exponentially with each passing quarter.

4. **Cybercab and Inflection Point Signals:** Bhakdi identifies the specific signals he is watching to become confident the robotaxi thesis is materializing: a new FSD version (referred to as 14.3) rolling out, likely within approximately two weeks from the video's recording; Cybercab units being deployed unsupervised without steering wheels; and multiple dozens of Cybercabs operating in cities. He states his personal inflection point threshold is approximately 2,000 fully commercial, unsupervised robotaxi vehicles operating 200 miles per day — at roughly 1,800 vehicles, he calculates this equals approximately 5% of Tesla's 2024 free cash flow, which he believes would force institutional investors to reprice the stock aggressively.

5. **Stock Repricing Mechanics:** Bhakdi explains his view that the stock repricing will not be a single event but a series of stair-steps as investors debate total addressable market. He envisions the stock repricing to $1,000, then pausing as investors debate whether Tesla can grow beyond replacing Uber, then repricing again to $2,000 as it captures car market share. He states his current fair value model for Tesla — if the robotaxi ramp executes as he projects — is approximately $800, with potential overshoot above $1,000 if tens of thousands of Cybercabs are deployed before year-end 2026.

## Autonomous Driving
1. **FSD Maturity and Real-World Sentiment Shift:** The host reports personally not touching his steering wheel for over a year, including a recent trip to Vancouver, Canada, describing FSD performance as essentially perfect in all conditions. He argues this represents a meaningful shift in consumer sentiment, with former skeptics now discovering and praising FSD. Bhakdi agrees but notes this is a double-edged dynamic — if FSD is truly that capable, it both drives car sales and unlocks the robotaxi business simultaneously, but failure in one likely means failure in both.

2. **Competitive FSD Landscape — BYD's God's Eye Struggles:** The video highlights that BYD's driver assistance system, called God's Eye, has drawn significant user complaints for poor mapping, slow performance, and general unreliability. This is cited as evidence that the "Tesla killer" narrative around BYD is overstated, particularly on the software side.

## Electric Vehicles
1. **Bear Case — HSBC's 65% Downside Warning:** HSBC is presented as the primary institutional bear, warning Tesla stock could fall as much as 65% from recent levels. Their argument centers on declining vehicle deliveries in 2025, a 5.5% year-over-year decrease in production, margin compression, and BYD competitive pressure. The host and Bhakdi largely dismiss this as the outdated "is Tesla just a car company" framing, though they acknowledge the car business is under real pressure.

2. **Tesla's Relative Strength as a Car Company:** Despite the bear narrative, Bhakdi argues Tesla is uniquely positioned even within the pure EV business. He notes Tesla operates manufacturing on all three major continents and is largely insulated from tariff disruptions affecting competitors. He contrasts Tesla's margins against Chinese EV makers, arguing BYD and others are competing at unsustainable margin levels. The host cites Tesla's US EV market share rebounding to approximately 60%, consistent with its historical share before other automakers ramped EV programs that have since been pulled back.

3. **BYD's Deteriorating Financials:** The video covers BYD's latest earnings miss, describing the company's chairman characterizing the current competitive environment as a "brutal knockout stage." BYD reported a 19% quarter-over-quarter profit decline in Q4, with multiple consecutive quarters of falling profitability despite rising sales volume — which Bhakdi interprets as a sign of dealer channel stuffing and unsustainable pricing. The host notes this undermines the HSBC bear case that frames BYD as Tesla's primary competitive threat.

## Market & Competition
1. **Institutional Analyst Credibility Gap:** The video criticizes traditional Wall Street analysts, using Canaccord analyst George Gianarikas as an example — he maintained a buy rating but lowered his price target from $520 to $420 without substantive new analysis, apparently following the stock price lower. Bhakdi argues retail analysts, Tesla-focused YouTubers, and ARK Invest have meaningfully more insight than generalist bank analysts who cover too many stocks to develop deep expertise in Tesla's technology transition.

2. **Macro and Geopolitical Risk as Near-Term Overlay:** Bhakdi describes his current portfolio positioning as minimized — at his smallest Tesla position in a long time — due to geopolitical tail risk, specifically referencing an Iran-related conflict situation. He states he is in the process of shifting back to aggressively bullish, potentially within one to two weeks of the video's recording, as he sees geopolitical tension easing and April catalysts (FSD 14.3, Cybercab deployment) approaching simultaneously. He frames this as needing to "front-run" the repricing before 2,000 robotaxis are deployed.

Notable quotes and data:
- Cathie Wood: "Tesla will ultimately win the lion's share... it's a little bit like Uber and Lyft. There's a network effect."
- Cathie Wood: Autonomous taxi market growing from ~$1 billion today to $10 trillion in 5–10 years; Tesla's gross margins could shift from 16% (EVs) to 80–90% (robotaxi).
- Cathie Wood: Tesla's costs will be 50% lower than Waymo's by 2030.
- Jo Bhakdi's inflection threshold: ~2,000 fully commercial, unsupervised robotaxi vehicles doing 200 miles/day = approximately 5% of Tesla's 2024 free cash flow.
- Bhakdi's fair value estimate for Tesla if robotaxi ramp executes: ~$800, with overshoot potential above $1,000.
- Bhakdi: "If Tesla solves it this year... they can deploy probably 500,000, maybe even a million of these things."
- Tesla US EV market share cited as having rebounded to approximately 60%.
- BYD reported a 19% Q4 profit decline; chairman described the environment as a "brutal knockout stage."
- HSBC warning: Tesla stock could fall as much as 65%; production declined 5.5% year-over-year.
- FSD version 14.3 described as likely releasing within approximately two weeks of recording.
- Canaccord price target lowered from $520 to $420 with no substantive new reasoning provided.

articles.title: Elon Says FSD v14.3 Will Feel Sentient Here's Why!
articles.body:
This video from the "brighterwithherbert" channel breaks down Elon Musk's announcement that FSD version 14.3 is in employee beta testing and expected to reach wide release by end of the week of April 2, 2026. The host argues that version 14.3 represents the most significant architectural upgrade since Tesla moved to end-to-end neural networks in version 12, driven by a 10x larger neural network and the addition of reasoning capabilities. The video connects this software milestone directly to Tesla's robotaxi rollout, framing version 14.3 as the final software piece needed to enable unsupervised autonomous driving at scale.

## Autonomous Driving

1. **FSD v14.3 in employee beta, wide release imminent:** Elon Musk announced that FSD version 14.3 entered employee beta testing as of the night before the video was published (around April 1–2, 2026). Wide release is expected by end of the week of April 2, 2026, though the host notes this could slip to early the following week. The host emphasizes this is confirmed fact, not speculation.

2. **10x larger neural network — a qualitative architectural leap:** Version 14.3 features a neural network with an order of magnitude more parameters than the current model running on Tesla vehicles. The host explains that more parameters allow the AI to recognize more patterns, handle more edge cases, and generalize to situations the previous model lacked capacity to learn — framing this not as incremental improvement but as a qualitative shift. The analogy used is that if the previous model understood 10,000 types of intersections, the new model could understand 100,000.

3. **Hardware 4 required for full version 14.3:** Because the 10x larger model requires significantly more compute and memory, version 14.3 targets hardware 4 vehicles first. Hardware 4 has double the memory of hardware 3 (hardware 4: 16 GB implied; hardware 3: 8 GB confirmed). The full model cannot run at full resolution on hardware 3.

4. **Reasoning as the "last big piece" for unsupervised autonomy:** The host identifies reasoning — the ability to plan ahead and evaluate context rather than merely react — as the defining new capability of version 14.3. Concrete examples given include: choosing optimal parking spots and navigating to them autonomously (enabling the "Banish" feature), handling construction zones where signage conflicts with road markings, and resolving ambiguous right-of-way situations like unprotected left turns and four-way stops. Ashok Swami, Tesla's VP of AI, confirmed that some reasoning capabilities already shipped in version 14.2, covering navigation route changes during construction and parking option evaluation, meaning version 14.3 represents a full deployment rather than a cold start.

5. **"Sentient" driving experience — what Elon likely means:** Elon posted in October (2025) that "by version 14.3, your car will feel like it's sentient." The host interprets this not as a claim of consciousness but as describing a driving experience so anticipatory and context-aware that it mimics a living being's understanding — slowing for a pedestrian based on body language before they step off the curb, proactively moving away from an erratically drifting driver, navigating a parking lot with full situational awareness. The host references a Tesla-posted video showing FSD predicting a pedestrian's intent to cross before they stepped off the curb as a preview of this behavior.

6. **Largest architectural change since version 12:** The host places version 14.3 in historical context: the shift from version 11 to version 12 was revolutionary (replacing ~300,000 lines of handcoded rules with an end-to-end neural network); version 12 to version 14 brought meaningful reliability gains but similar model size; version 14.3 is described as a different category of upgrade entirely due to the 10x model size increase paired with reasoning and reinforcement learning.

7. **Additional expected features in version 14.3:** Beyond reasoning and the larger model, the host outlines several expected improvements: (1) **Banish/reverse summon** — the car drops you at the door and parks itself autonomously, described as technically feasible for the first time due to reasoning; (2) **Improved Smart Summon** — current summon still runs on older version 12/Autopilot stacks, so upgrading to the v14.3 architecture should improve reliability significantly; (3) **Better navigation and route planning** — applying reasoning to construction detours and optimal routing; (4) **Improved performance on unmapped and private roads** — handling environments the car has never seen; (5) **Improved performance in rain, fog, and degraded conditions** — an acknowledged weakness flagged as an area NHTSA is investigating.

8. **FSD "Lite" for hardware 3 vehicles expected in Q2 2026:** Ashok Swami confirmed during Tesla's earnings call that FSD version 14 Lite — a distilled or quantized (compressed) version of the version 14 architecture — is in development for hardware 3 vehicles and is expected in Q2 2026, meaning by end of June 2026. The host compares it to streaming a movie in 720p vs. 4K: same architecture, less detail. Whether the reasoning layer will be included in the Lite version is explicitly uncertain, as the host suspects reasoning components may be what gets cut to fit hardware 3's memory constraints. The host personally expects the Lite release no earlier than late June 2026, and notes a "more likely" scenario of it slipping later.

9. **Tesla's cumulative FSD safety data:** The host states Tesla's safety data shows FSD is approximately 7 to 11 times safer than a human driver on a per-mile basis. The Tesla fleet has logged 8.4 billion miles of FSD data.

10. **Unsupervised FSD already activated for non-employee owners:** Tesla activated unsupervised FSD for non-employee owners in a geo-fenced area in Austin on March 18, 2026 — the car drives itself with no human monitoring, though limited to a small defined area.

## Robotaxi

1. **Version 14.3 is the software powering Cybercab and the robotaxi rollout:** The host frames version 14.3 as directly enabling the robotaxi business. Cybercab — described as purpose-built with no steering wheel or pedals and priced under $30,000 — is stated to be entering production this month (April 2026) and will run version 14.3 from day one.

2. **Tesla hiring AI safety operators in 18 cities across 12 states:** The host notes Tesla is actively hiring AI safety operators in 18 cities across 12 states, interpreting this as evidence of an active rollout rather than a research project, and linking it directly to version 14.3 as the software that makes that expansion possible.

3. **The five-piece robotaxi puzzle:** The host outlines Elon's framework for the robotaxi buildout as five pieces: (1) End-to-end neural network (shipped in v12); (2) Scale and safety (achieved through v14 to v14.2, with 8.4 billion fleet miles logged); (3) Unsupervised capability (activated in Austin geo-fence, March 18, 2026); (4) Reasoning for complex scenarios (coming with v14.3); (5) Purpose-built hardware (Cybercab production starting April 2026). The host argues that with version 14.3, the architecture is complete enough to scale — future updates are iteration, not foundational change.

4. **Q1 2026 earnings call flagged as key investor catalyst:** The host instructs investors to mark the Q1 2026 earnings call in late April 2026, specifically flagging forward guidance on FSD version 14.3 reception, Cybercab integration, and unsupervised expansion plans as the most important data points for the quarter.

## Electric Vehicles

1. **Cybercab specifications and pricing:** The Cybercab is described as a purpose-built vehicle with no steering wheel and no pedals, priced under $30,000, with production beginning in April 2026. It will run FSD version 14.3 from day one.

## Market & Competition

1. **Hardware 3 owner frustration as a market dynamic:** The host acknowledges that hardware 3 owners who paid $12,000 for FSD will experience frustration at receiving a lesser version of the software due to chip constraints. This is flagged as a real reputational and customer satisfaction issue for Tesla, even as the host accepts it as a technical inevitability given that the AI has "outgrown" the older chip.

Notable quotes and data:
- Elon Musk (October post): *"By version 14.3, your car will feel like it's sentient."*
- FSD v14.3 neural network: **10x larger** (order of magnitude more parameters) than the current model
- FSD v14.3 targets **hardware 4 vehicles first**; hardware 3 has 8 GB memory, hardware 4 has double that
- Tesla FSD safety data: approximately **7 to 11 times safer than a human driver** per mile
- Tesla fleet FSD miles logged: **8.4 billion miles**
- Unsupervised FSD activated for non-employee owners in Austin geo-fence: **March 18, 2026**
- Cybercab priced **under $30,000**, no steering wheel, no pedals, production starting **April 2026**
- Tesla hiring AI safety operators in **18 cities across 12 states**
- FSD Lite for hardware 3 expected: **Q2 2026 (by end of June 2026)**, per Ashok Swami
- FSD paid: hardware 3 owners paid **$12,000** for Full Self-Driving
- End-to-end neural network in v12 replaced **300,000 lines of handcoded rules**
- Wide release of v14.3 expected: **end of week of April 2, 2026** (could slip to early following week)
- Q1 2026 earnings call expected: **late April 2026**

articles.title: Tesla: $10T or Down 60%?
articles.body:
This video features a discussion between the host and Jo Bhakdi, an economist, investor, and tech entrepreneur, framing Tesla's investment case as a binary bet between a $10 trillion bull scenario and a 60%+ crash bear scenario. The core argument is that the bears are correct about pressure on Tesla's core car business, but wrong to ignore the transformative potential of robotaxi, Optimus, and AI compute. The debate ultimately centers on whether Tesla can execute on autonomous driving fast enough to establish an insurmountable network effect before competitors catch up.

## Autonomous Driving

1. **FSD as the Critical Inflection Point:** Jo Bhakdi argues that 2026 is potentially the pivotal year for FSD reaching commercial robotaxi viability. He states that all observable facts point to April or May being an inflection point, with FSD version 14.3 expected within approximately two weeks of the recording. The host corroborates this qualitatively, noting he has not touched his steering wheel in over a year and recently completed a trip to Vancouver, Canada without any manual intervention.

2. **Two-Year Window Before Competition Becomes Serious:** Jo Bhakdi frames Tesla's competitive advantage as time-sensitive. If Tesla solves commercial unsupervised FSD in 2026, he argues it is a winner-take-most outcome because no competitor — including Waymo — can scale at the same speed or cost. However, if Tesla is delayed by roughly two years (to approximately 2028), other large OEMs such as Volkswagen and Toyota, aided by Nvidia's autonomous driving partnerships, will have had sufficient time to develop credible competing platforms. He notes that Nvidia is actively working with all major OEMs, making the window for Tesla's first-mover advantage finite.

3. **Early Signal Watching:** Jo Bhakdi specifies the signals he is watching to confirm the thesis is playing out: FSD 14.3 receiving widespread positive user reports on X, Cybercab units rolling out unsupervised in meaningful numbers (described as "multiple dozens"), and expansion to additional cities. He emphasizes the need to "front run" these catalysts rather than wait for full confirmation.

## Robotaxi

1. **2,000 Robotaxis as the Repricing Forcing Function:** Jo Bhakdi has a specific threshold he has maintained consistently: approximately 2,000 robotaxis fully commercially deployed, unsupervised, driving 200 miles per day. He calculates that at roughly 1,800 units at that utilization level, robotaxi revenue would represent approximately 5% of Tesla's 2024 free cash flow — enough to demonstrate a 10–20% monthly growth rate and force institutional investors to reprice the stock dramatically.

2. **Fleet Scale Target for Meaningful Stock Re-Rating:** Beyond the initial 2,000-unit inflection point, Jo Bhakdi suggests that if Tesla scales to "multiple tens of thousands" of Cybercabs — citing 20,000 as a hypothetical example — before the end of 2026, the stock could at minimum double from its current level and potentially reach $1,000. He states his DCF model currently shows a fair value of approximately $800 if the robotaxi business scales as he projects, with upside overshoot possible due to market enthusiasm.

3. **Network Effects Lock In the Market:** Both the host and Jo Bhakdi agree with Cathie Wood's framing that robotaxi is a winner-take-most market driven by network effects — lower wait times, higher fleet density, and stronger brand recognition compounding over time. Jo Bhakdi adds that by approximately 2029, if Tesla has established sufficient scale, late-entering competitors simply cannot compete on a network density level regardless of their manufacturing capabilities.

4. **Trucking as an Underappreciated TAM:** Jo Bhakdi raises autonomous trucking as a significantly underappreciated opportunity, suggesting it roughly doubles the total addressable market beyond passenger robotaxi. He notes this is "all Tesla's territory" if the underlying autonomy technology works.

5. **Cathie Wood's Robotaxi Bull Case:** Cathie Wood is cited as projecting the autonomous taxi market growing from approximately $1 billion today to a $10 trillion opportunity within 5 to 10 years, encompassing not just ride-hailing but full personal transportation replacement — particularly when per-mile costs drop below $1. She projects Tesla's costs will be 50% lower than Waymo's by 2030 due to vertical integration, and that robotaxi gross margins could reach 80–90% compared to Tesla's current ~16% EV gross margins.

## Electric Vehicles

1. **Bear Case on the Car Business Has Merit But Is Overstated:** The HSBC bear case — that Tesla stock could crash 65% — is based on vehicle deliveries declining in 2025, production down 5.5% year-over-year, no new vehicle launches since the Cybertruck, and competitive pressure. Jo Bhakdi acknowledges the car business is under pressure but argues HSBC's assessment is flawed because it treats Tesla purely as a car company and ignores robotaxi, Optimus, and AI compute entirely.

2. **Tesla's US EV Market Share Rebounded to 60%:** The host notes that Tesla's share of US EV sales has rebounded to approximately 60%, matching its early market position, as other EV manufacturers have pulled back from the segment. This is cited as evidence that the competitive pressure narrative from HSBC is overstated.

3. **Tesla's Manufacturing Margins Are Structurally Superior:** Jo Bhakdi argues that Tesla's core strength — even as a pure car company — is its manufacturing efficiency, describing it as "the machine that builds the machine." He contends Tesla produces cars at margins far beyond what Chinese competitors can achieve, and that BYD's model of high sales volume at declining profitability validates this point.

## Market & Competition

1. **BYD Entering a Brutal Competitive Phase:** BYD is cited as missing earnings and entering what its own chairman described as a "fever pitch" and "brutal knockout stage." BYD reported a 19% quarter-over-quarter profit decline in Q4, with profitability dropping across multiple consecutive quarters despite rising sales volumes. The host raises additional skepticism about whether BYD's reported sales figures are inflated by channel stuffing through its dealer network.

2. **BYD's God's Eye FSD Equivalent Is Struggling:** BYD's driver-assist system, called "God's Eye," is reported to have drawn user complaints for poor mapping, slow response, and generally not working well — undermining the narrative that BYD poses a serious near-term threat to Tesla's autonomy lead.

3. **Traditional OEMs Face Tariff and Margin Pressure:** Jo Bhakdi notes that all major global automakers are under severe pressure from Chinese competition with unsustainable margins, combined with tariff protections enacted by both the EU and US. He argues Tesla is uniquely positioned as the only automaker manufacturing on three continents with minimal tariff exposure and strong margins relative to peers.

4. **Canaccord Analyst Price Target Reduction Criticized:** Canaccord analyst George is highlighted as reducing his Tesla price target from $520 to $420 while maintaining a buy rating, without substantive explanation beyond the stock price decline. Both the host and Jo Bhakdi criticize this as a reflexive, price-anchored adjustment rather than genuine fundamental analysis, contrasting it unfavorably with ARK Invest's more detailed scenario modeling.

5. **Institutional Analysts Losing Edge to Retail Research Communities:** Jo Bhakdi argues that retail and community-driven research platforms — citing Pioneer Lands and the broader "Tesla tuber" community — now have a structural information advantage over bank analysts who cover too many stocks to develop deep expertise in any one company, particularly one with as many moving parts as Tesla.

Notable quotes and data:
- Cathie Wood: autonomous taxi market growing from ~$1 billion today to a $10 trillion opportunity in 5–10 years
- Cathie Wood: robotaxi gross margins of 80–90% vs. current EV gross margins of ~16%
- Cathie Wood: Tesla's costs will be 50% lower than Waymo's by 2030
- HSBC: Tesla stock could crash 65%; Tesla production down 5.5% year-over-year in 2025
- Jo Bhakdi's inflection threshold: ~2,000 robotaxis deployed unsupervised at 200 miles/day ≈ 5% of Tesla's 2024 free cash flow
- Jo Bhakdi's fair value estimate if robotaxi scales as projected: ~$800/share, with potential overshoot to $1,000+
- Jo Bhakdi's scenario: 20,000 Cybercabs deployed by end of 2026 could cause stock to "at minimum double"
- FSD version 14.3 expected within approximately two weeks of recording (early April 2026)
- Tesla's US EV market share: ~60%, back to early-market levels
- BYD Q4 profit decline: 19% quarter-over-quarter
- Canaccord price target: reduced from $520 to $420, buy rating maintained; Q1 delivery estimate raised to 370,000 from 367,000
- Jo Bhakdi: "If 2026 is the year this happens, it's game over. Tesla is going to take this entire thing over."
- Jo Bhakdi: "I think all the facts point to April or May being an inflection point."

articles.title: We Looked Into Donut Battery So You Don't Have To
articles.body:
This video is a conversation between host Brian (Futurazza) and Jordan Gizigi from "The Limiting Factor," a battery technology analyst, recorded on April 1st. The discussion centers on Donut Labs, a startup that had promised delivery-ready solid-state batteries in customers' hands by Q1 2026, and why that promise — like so many "miracle battery" announcements before it — failed to materialize. The core thesis is that the gap between laboratory battery performance and real-world commercial viability is enormous, and that Donut Labs has been engaging in selective, misleading marketing rather than transparent scientific disclosure.

## Electric Vehicles

1. **Donut Labs' unfulfilled Q1 promise:** Donut Labs had publicly announced that delivery-ready solid-state batteries would be in customer hands by Q1 2026, promising 400 watt hours per kilogram energy density and five-minute charging, targeted at electric motorcycles. The host says he expressed skepticism from the start, and as of April 1st, no delivery has occurred. The Wall Street Journal had run a headline asking "Will this miracle battery finally change your mind about EVs?" amplifying the hype around the claims.

2. **Battery specs claimed at CES were likely an "OR" list, not an "AND" list:** Jordan explains that Donut Labs presented a "laundry list of the highest performing specs we've ever seen in any battery" at CES. His assessment is that under certain laboratory conditions, the company can probably meet each spec individually with heavy caveats, but cannot hit all specifications simultaneously. A recent test cycled a cell brought up to 100 degrees Celsius, and after approximately 10 to 50 cycles, the battery was only providing 50% capacity — far short of the advertised performance.

3. **Lab-to-real-world manufacturing gap:** Jordan uses the analogy of making shoes by hand versus manufacturing them in a factory at high volumes and low cost. He explains that batteries placed into actual products must meet durability requirements across all temperature and weather conditions, function within a pack shared by hundreds of other cells, and sustain performance for over a decade. This is fundamentally different from what can be demonstrated in a controlled lab setting.

4. **Donut Labs' April 1st video walkback:** On the morning of the video recording, Donut Labs released a new video in which the founder acknowledged that the battery that had been tested is not actually the final battery, and that a version two with "much better performance" is coming. He also stated that cycle life is complicated and that skeptics wouldn't believe the data anyway — citing over 100% Coulombic efficiency on the first few cycles as a misunderstood proof point. Jordan explains this is a well-understood, mechanically obvious phenomenon related to lithium ion behavior in anode overhangs during battery formation and is not evidence of superior performance.

5. **Donut Labs may be a middleman, not a manufacturer:** Jordan speculates that Donut Labs may not actually be producing the batteries themselves, but rather purchasing lab samples from another company that is the actual manufacturer. In this interpretation, the Donut Labs founder may have genuinely been sold on the technology and is acting as a middleman between a battery manufacturer and the end customer (Verge, an electric motorcycle company), rather than knowingly perpetrating a fraud.

6. **Semi-solid state batteries already in market — MG4:** In contrast to Donut Labs' undelivered promises, the video notes that the MG4 is described as the world's first mass-market EV with a semi-solid state battery and is entering the European market. Jordan explains that semi-solid state batteries use a small amount of gel or "jelly" between layers to address the interface contact problem in fully solid-state batteries, where two "brick-like" solid layers cannot achieve perfect contact regardless of pressure.

7. **QuantumCape as a more credible comparison:** Jordan references QuantumCape as a better-funded, more credible solid-state battery developer. QuantumCape originally targeted around 400 watt hours per kilogram energy density, and what they have now is in the range of just above 300 watt hours per kilogram. Despite being better-resourced and more transparent, it will be approximately seven years after their original announcement before a commercial product is seen.

8. **Solid-state batteries are a 10–15 year horizon, not a step change:** Jordan's assessment is that solid-state batteries may become a genuine improvement on liquid electrolyte batteries in approximately 10 to 15 years, but the transition will be gradual rather than a sudden step change. He notes that current solid-state batteries do offer range improvements but come with significant additional costs, and that every battery chemistry involves trade-offs. Progress in the field comes from tens of thousands of scientists and engineers making incremental molecular-level improvements over time.

9. **"Miracle battery" announcements are endemic:** Jordan notes that miracle battery announcements appear roughly two to three times per week across the industry. Every battery startup must claim superiority over existing technology to attract investment, leading to a constant stream of overclaimed performance specs. Technologies like LFP (lithium iron phosphate) and sodium-ion, once dismissed by skeptics, have proven viable — though sodium-ion research slowed when lithium prices fell. Sodium-ion batteries are now appearing in passenger vehicles in China with growing adoption.

## Market & Competition

1. **Selective marketing versus scientific fraud:** The hosts debate whether Donut Labs' founder is lying or engaged in selective truth-telling for marketing purposes. Jordan's interpretation, based on the founder's behavior in online interactions and his April 1st video, is that the founder views this as a "marketing game" — giving selective truths to generate excitement without technically lying outright, while consistently omitting critical caveats and withholding data that would qualify his claims. Brian draws a parallel to Thomas Edison's investor demonstrations with incandescent bulbs (swapping bulbs between investor presentations) and Steve Jobs' iPhone demonstration (using different phones between demo steps to avoid failure).

2. **No identified victims, no clear legal liability:** The hosts agree that as long as Donut Labs has not accepted new donations or investments since the CES show, there may be no legal victims and therefore no crime — just a "social experiment." The question of who would have legal standing to bring a case is raised but left unresolved.

Notable quotes and data:
- Donut Labs promised 400 watt hours per kilogram energy density and five-minute charging for electric motorcycles by Q1 2026.
- After approximately 10–50 cycles at 100°C, the Donut Labs test cell was providing only 50% capacity.
- QuantumCape originally targeted ~400 Wh/kg; current performance is described as "just above 300" Wh/kg, approximately seven years after their original announcement.
- Jordan: "Every battery comes with trade-offs — you have to pay the piper one way or another."
- Jordan: "Maybe in 10 or 15 years we'll have solid-state batteries that are an improvement on liquid electrolyte batteries."
- Brian: "The checklist was long... it checked too many boxes to not strain credulity."
- Jordan on Donut Labs' team size: "I think they have one [person] and I'm not sure that it's actually their product. I think they bought it."
- Jordan on miracle battery frequency: "I would see them two, three times a week."
- The MG4 is described as the world's first mass-market EV with a semi-solid state battery, entering the European market.
- Shirley Mung is described as head of battery research at Argonne National Laboratories and a student of Nobel Prize-winning battery researchers.

articles.title: Q1 Tesla Delivery Numbers RELEASED - Live @5:40am Pacific
articles.body:
This is a live stream from April 2, 2026, hosted by Brian ("futureaza"), covering the real-time release of Tesla's Q1 2026 production and delivery numbers. The video begins as a pre-release watch party with viewer predictions and analyst estimates, then shifts to reaction once the numbers drop. The core finding is a significant delivery miss relative to most forecasts, with production coming in above deliveries by a wide margin.

## Electric Vehicles

1. **Q1 2026 Official Results — Delivery Miss, Production Near Estimates:** Tesla reported Q1 2026 total deliveries of 358,023 vehicles against production of 408,577 vehicles. Brian had personally estimated 382,000 deliveries and 392,000 produced, meaning he missed deliveries by a wide margin but production by only about 2,611 units (roughly half a percent). The Wall Street analyst consensus was approximately 365,645 deliveries, making the actual result a meaningful miss even against the Street.

2. **Large Production-Delivery Gap Raises Questions:** The roughly 50,000-unit gap between production and deliveries — with more units produced than delivered — drew viewer discussion about whether surplus inventory is being held for the nascent robotaxi fleet, directed into a rental fleet, or simply represents vehicles in transit. Brian acknowledged the gap is unusual but characterized the energy storage deployment shortfall similarly — as inventory that will flow through in future quarters.

3. **Giga Texas Shutdown — Model Y Long (Y L) Retooling:** Brian expressed high confidence that the Giga Texas production shutdown during the week of the report is related to Model Y Long (Y L) retooling. He noted that most of the tooling work was already completed in a prior worldwide factory shutdown last year, and that Y L production movement has also been observed at Giga Berlin. He pushed back on critics who say the Model Y isn't big enough, pointing out it remains the world's best-selling EV despite four factory shutdowns for retooling globally.

4. **Model Y Global Sales Performance — Regional Data Points:** Third-party monthly data cited by analyst Roland Percher gave Brian additional pre-release confidence in his estimates. South Korea reported approximately 11,000 Tesla sales in March alone, making Tesla the third best-selling brand behind Hyundai and Kia. Giga Shanghai wholesale figures came in at approximately 85,000 units in March. Italy reported approximately 2,920 sales for March. European weekly sales of approximately 6,400 were also noted. Spain and France were described as performing well in Q1.

5. **SNX (Model S/X) Discontinuation — Inventory Clearing:** Brian noted that SNX production has ended, with only limited remaining inventory. He advised viewers who have been waiting to buy a Model S or Model X to act quickly before remaining units sell out.

## Energy

1. **Q1 Energy Deployment — Below Expectations:** Energy storage deployment came in at 8.8 gigawatt hours for Q1 2026.
