He frames this as a ~1% weekly alpha target if Tesla stays above $400. He is explicitly avoiding selling short calls because he remains bullish on the medium-term outlook and does not want to cap his upside if good news arrives unexpectedly. He states he has fully exited LEAP positions, having entered around $235–$240 and exited in the $420–$480 range.

Notable quotes and data:
- Robotaxi tracker at 386 vehicles at time of recording; host expects to cross 400 "over the weekend"
- Trajectory forecast: 450 (end of next week) → 900 (March) → 1,800 (April)
- Forcing function defined as: 1,800 robotaxis × 200 miles/day × $1/mile margin
- Tesla stock declined ~25% in Q1, from ~$490 to ~$395
- Host selling 410 short puts collecting ~$8–$9; break-even at $401
- Joe Tegtmeyer spotted approximately 3 new Cybercab production-line units "in the wild"
- Host notes 50% underutilization of robotaxis would push the effective forcing function threshold to ~3,600 vehicles
- "The maximum weakness of Q1 will be probably the first two weeks of March" — host's tactical forecast

2026-02-21.title: If You Buy a CYBERCAB How Much MONEY Could You Make?
2026-02-21.body:
This video features Herbert (channel host) and Sern Basher, a Chartered Financial Analyst who runs the investment advisory firm Brilliant Advice, discussing the financial economics of buying a Tesla Cybercab as a personal income-generating asset. The conversation is anchored in a detailed financial model Basher built, which went viral on X with nearly a million views. Their central thesis is that at under $30,000, a Cybercab represents an entirely new asset class — a depreciating vehicle transformed into an ROI-driven income machine — and that the economics are compelling even under the most conservative assumptions.

Key Points

1. Elon Musk has publicly confirmed Cybercab will be sold to consumers for under $30,000 before end of 2026. Musk responded directly to a challenge from Dylan Loomis on X, confirming that individual customers would be able to purchase a Cybercab at that price point before 2027. This prompted Basher to model what the economics would actually look like for an individual owner.

2. Tesla has multiple strategic reasons to sell Cybercabs to individuals rather than operating the entire fleet itself. First, Uber and Lyft drivers could buy Cybercabs and quit ride-share, accelerating the transition away from human-driven ride-hail. Second, selling vehicles to consumers reduces Tesla's capital intensity — they build the car, sell it for a profit, and recycle that capital into more production. Third, and perhaps most importantly, creating thousands or millions of Cybercab owners creates a political constituency that will resist regulatory attacks on Tesla's autonomy business, mirroring the strategy Uber used with its drivers.

3. A key Tesla job posting confirms dynamic and surge pricing is planned for the robo-taxi network. Tesla is hiring a robo-taxi pricing software engineer tasked with designing algorithms incorporating base costs, taxes, tolls, market demand, and regional factors. The job description explicitly mentions "dynamic and surge pricing," signaling that a flat dollar-per-mile rate is a long-term aspiration, not a launch-day reality.

4. Current ride-share pricing context makes even a $1/mile Cybercab fare look dramatically cheap. A June 2024 study of Waymo, Uber, and Lyft pricing in San Francisco showed per-mile costs ranging from $4–$42 for short trips under a mile, and $4–$6 per mile even for trips between 2.7 and 5.8 miles. The model conservatively uses $1/mile, which Basher notes is already in use in Austin, Texas, and likely well below where pricing will actually start.

5. The core financial model uses specific assumptions to project owner income. Key inputs include: 25 mph average travel speed, 5-mile average trip length, 40% empty (repositioning) miles, 3-minute average loading/unloading time, Tesla's take rate fixed at 35%, 600,000-mile battery life (conservative versus a potential 1 million miles), $199/month FSD subscription, utilization rates ranging from 30% to 65%, $1 base fare plus $1/mile plus $0.42/minute idle charge, and home charging at $0.16/kWh.

6. At 30% vehicle utilization — the worst-case scenario — an individual owner generates roughly $32,000 in gross revenue annually, yielding approximately $14,000 in gross profit after all costs. Costs include FSD ($2,400/year), charging ($2,300/year), tires ($2,800/year), vehicle depreciation/financing, EV fees (~$1,300/year), and miscellaneous expenses. Cleaning and parking are zeroed out assuming home-based ownership but noted as variables.

7. Gross profit scales dramatically with utilization. At 35% utilization (8.5 hours/day active), gross profit rises modestly. At 65% utilization (15.6 hours/day), gross profit approaches $40,000–$41,000 per year. Variable costs like tires and vehicle depreciation increase with mileage, but fixed costs like financing remain constant, improving margins at higher utilization.

8. After a 35% tax rate, net profit at 30% utilization is approximately $8,000/year, meaning the car pays for itself in roughly 3.5 years. At 35% utilization the payback period drops to about 3 years. At 65% utilization, net profit approaches $25,000–$26,000 — nearly recovering the entire $30,000 purchase price in a single year.

9. The model also works in dense urban scenarios (15 mph, 3-mile trips, supercharger pricing at $0.35/kWh) and rural scenarios (45 mph, 25-mile trips, 100% empty return miles, $5 base fare). In urban settings, gross profit drops only slightly — from $14,000 to ~$13,000 at 30% utilization. In rural settings, gross profit at 30% utilization is approximately $21,000, rising to $57,000 at 65% utilization, demonstrating the model's robustness across geographies.

10. Tesla's optimal take rate, based on fairness modeling, appears to be 30–35%. At a 15% take rate, the owner earns 269% more than Tesla — clearly unsustainable for Tesla. At 25% (which Musk once cited), owners still earn 1.7x what Tesla earns. At 30–35%, the split approaches rough parity, with owners earning 0–25% more than Tesla depending on utilization. Basher argues this is the likely equilibrium range, with the rate potentially being dynamic rather than fixed.

11. FSD pricing is one of three levers Tesla can adjust to balance economics. Tesla could offer FSD for free and raise the take rate, charge $199/month and use a 35% take rate, or charge $999/month and lower the take rate to 15–20%. At $2/mile pricing, even a $1,000/month FSD fee with a 30–35% take rate remains fair for both parties. The key insight is that Tesla has enormous flexibility to optimize across vehicle price, FSD cost, and take rate simultaneously.

12. Tesla's fleet-scale economics are staggering even at $1/mile. With 10 million Cybercabs at 65% utilization, Tesla could generate approximately $250 billion in net income. At 50 million vehicles (estimated US need), net income approaches $1 trillion. At 100 million globally, net income could reach $1–2 trillion. Applying a conservative P/E of 30, that implies a valuation of $2.5 trillion to $70 trillion for the robo-taxi business alone — not including Optimus or other revenue streams.

13. A poll of 1,147 X users revealed extraordinary consumer demand. Only 7% said they would not buy a Cybercab. 20% would buy one, 37.5% would buy two to five, and 35% would buy more than five. Extrapolating, that sample of 1,000 respondents could have generated 3,000 to 8,500 individual orders if ordering were open today.

Conclusions and Recommendations

Basher recommends thinking of Cybercab ownership as analogous to rental real estate — a capital income asset rather than wage income — representing a historic structural shift. He suggests early buyers will benefit most, as fares will likely be higher than $1/mile at launch and will compress over time as competition and scale increase. He personally plans to convert his existing 74 Cybertruck orders into Cybercab orders. His closing advice: investors can hedge by owning both Tesla stock and a Cybercab, capturing upside regardless of whether Tesla or individual owners capture more of the economics.

Notable Quotes and Data

"For the first time in history, we're taking a depreciating asset and turning it into a return on investment." — Sern Basher

"The economic opportunity is so large it makes sense to share the wealth. If you don't share the wealth, you are a target and you will be destroyed." — Basher on why Tesla will sell to consumers

At 30% utilization / $1 per mile: ~$32,000 gross revenue, ~$14,000 gross profit, ~$8,000 net after 35% tax
At 65% utilization / $1 per mile: ~$40,000+ gross profit, ~$25,000–$26,000 net
Rural scenario at 65% utilization: ~$57,000 gross profit
Poll result: 35% of ~1,147 respondents would buy more than 5 Cybercabs
Estimated US fleet needed long-term: 50 million vehicles
Global fleet scenario net income potential: $1–2 trillion annually

2026-02-21.title: Tesla Pulls Cybertruck Surprise
2026-02-21.body:
Tesla has introduced a new base trim Cybertruck priced at $59,990 (approximately $60,000 before destination fees), representing a $20,000 price cut from the previous standard model. The host, Brian from Futurza, walks through what buyers get and lose at this price point, critiques media coverage of the announcement, and provides broader context on Cybertruck sales history and Tesla's business situation. His central argument is that this is a genuinely good value proposition, especially when adjusted for inflation.

1. The new base Cybertruck is priced at $59,990 and retains the dual motor all-wheel drive powertrain. Unlike the failed rear-wheel drive variant from the previous year, this truck keeps the performance fundamentals intact. It still does 0-60 mph in 4 seconds and offers 325 miles of range.

2. What the base trim loses compared to the premium is relatively minor. Omissions include air suspension, the rear passenger touchscreen, leatherette upholstery (replaced with cloth), ventilated front seats, two in-cabin power outlets, and a downgraded 7-speaker sound system instead of 15. Active noise cancellation is also removed. Notably, the motorized tonneau cover and bed power outlets are retained, which the host flags as critical for utility buyers.

3. The towing capacity drops from 11,000 lbs to 7,500 lbs on the new base trim, though the reason for this reduction is unclear. Wheel size also goes from 20-inch to 18-inch, with a $2,500 upgrade option available if buyers want larger wheels.

4. When adjusted for inflation, the $60,000 price is actually cheaper than the original 2019 unveiling price. When the Cybertruck was revealed in 2019, the dual motor version was priced at $50,000, which equals approximately $63,500 in today's dollars. The host cites a post from Matthew Donigan Ryan to make this comparison. Given that the air suspension was never part of the original promise and the rear screen and ventilated seats were additions added later, the host argues buyers are essentially getting the originally promised truck for less than the originally promised price.

5. For additional context, the host compares to the Ford F-150 Raptor, which started at $47,000 in 2019 and now starts at $79,000, illustrating that all trucks have gotten significantly more expensive while the Cybertruck has held its price relatively well.

6. The offer is time-limited, potentially only 10 days. Elon Musk responded to a post on X stating "Only for the next 10 days," but when asked for clarification added "depends on how much demand we see at this price level." The host speculates the 10-day deadline may be a soft corporate pressure tactic rather than a hard deadline.

7. The host revisits and dismisses the failed rear-wheel drive Cybertruck variant launched and canceled within 6 months in the prior year. That truck was priced at $70,000, lacked bed outlets, had no powered tonneau cover, no adaptive suspension, no rear light bar, and only offered 350 miles of range on a single motor. Even Tesla employees the host spoke with couldn't articulate who the target buyer was. He contrasts this clearly inferior and more expensive option with the new base trim to make the point that this new version is far better executed.

8. The host challenges several claims made in media coverage, particularly from what he describes as poor-quality articles. He disputes the framing of Tesla facing "severe business struggles," noting that Cybertruck production capacity is around 250,000 units annually while only about 25,000 are being built. He also pushes back on claims of brand damage affecting Tesla in China, arguing that Chinese consumers don't consume Western media and that Giga Shanghai continues to perform strongly.

9. On the topic of Tesla's financials, the host objects to excluding regulatory credits from automotive margin calculations. He argues that regulatory credits have been a core part of Tesla's business model since the beginning and that stripping them out to show the automotive segment "dipping into the red" is analytically misleading.

10. Cybertruck sales data cited in the coverage showed approximately 5,400 units sold in Q3, a 62% year-over-year drop, compared to Ford's Lightning selling roughly 10,000 in the same period before discontinuation. The host notes Ford's figures represent dealer lot shipments rather than actual customer deliveries, characterizing it as channel stuffing.

Notable quotes or data: Elon Musk on X: "Only for the next 10 days" and "depends on how much demand we see at this price level." Original 2019 dual motor Cybertruck price: $50,000, equivalent to $63,500 adjusted for inflation. Ford Raptor price increase: $47,000 in 2019 to $79,000 now. Tesla service revenues cited in the article: $12.7 billion. Cybertruck Q3 sales figure cited: approximately 5,400 units, down 62% year-over-year.

The host's overall recommendation is that the $60,000 base Cybertruck is a legitimate value proposition for buyers who don't need air suspension and can live without luxury interior features. He encourages interested buyers to act within the 10-day window and mentions his referral code offers free FSD trial months.

2026-02-21.title: Cybercab Charging Approved, Cybertruck Gets Cheap & Big Bad Tunnel Time - LIVE!
2026-02-21.body:
This is episode 134 of the Tesla Beat, a weekly live discussion hosted by the Futureaza channel covering the latest Tesla and related technology news. The panel — regular hosts Brian and Hans, along with guest Matthew DR and co-host Joe — covers Cybercab production milestones, a new budget Cybertruck variant, wireless charging regulatory approval, FSD performance data, and the Boring Company's Nashville tunnel approval. The overarching theme is that Tesla's autonomous vehicle ecosystem is converging rapidly, with multiple pieces — hardware, software, charging infrastructure, and regulatory approvals — falling into place simultaneously.

1. Cybercab production has officially begun at Giga Texas. The first production Cybercab rolled off the line, with footage circulating of it going around the facility. The panel views this as a significant signal: starting a production line is not something done two years before launch — it indicates Tesla has sufficient confidence in the underlying autonomy software for imminent deployment. Production is on track to begin in earnest in April 2026.

2. The launch implies a potential for the fastest vehicle scale-up in Tesla's history. Once production is optimized and the robotaxi platform can operate without remote monitors, the panel argues there is essentially unlimited demand for Tesla to deploy these vehicles itself rather than sell them to consumers. One panelist speculates Tesla may only sell a handful publicly, perhaps geofenced to Austin, since operating a single Cybercab in a fleet could recover its cost in roughly six months.

3. A new, more affordable all-wheel drive Cybertruck variant was announced, starting at $59,990. This is $20,000 less than the premium version. Key omissions include air suspension, the vault bed cover, cloth seats instead of vegan leather, a seven-speaker rather than nine-speaker audio system, and no premium center console. Everything else — four-wheel steering, steer-by-wire, power share, AWD, the large lockable frunk storage — is retained. Elon Musk subsequently tweeted that the pricing is only available for 10 days, which the panel interprets as either a demand-sensing test or a signal that the price will increase by $3,000–$5,000 afterward.

4. The value proposition of this Cybertruck was extensively benchmarked against competitors. A comparable XLT F-150 Super Crew at a local Ford dealership was quoted at $67,000 MSRP before dealer fees. The F-150 Raptor, which was $47,000 in 2019, now costs $79,000. The original 2019 Cybertruck AWD announcement price of $49,900 is equivalent to roughly $63,000 in 2026 dollars. The panel argues Cybertruck pricing has risen less than comparable ICE trucks and that ongoing fuel savings make the total cost of ownership significantly lower, especially for contractors who would benefit from power share.

5. The FCC granted Tesla a waiver to use ultra-wideband radio technology for its wireless EV charging system, specifically designed for the Cybercab. Normally this technology must be handheld or installed in fixed outdoor equipment under specific conditions; Tesla's charging pad required an exception. The panel notes this is critical infrastructure for running a fully autonomous fleet — eliminating the need for human intervention to plug in vehicles. Matthew DR added important technical context: Tesla has been pursuing a wireless charging frequency different from the SAE international standard, which he believes is the real reason wireless charging was quietly dropped from the Cybertruck (not vehicle height as Tesla stated). This FCC approval covers the UWB alignment component; the charging frequency standard issue remains unresolved.

6. Tesla announced 8 billion cumulative FSD miles driven. The panel notes that Elon had previously cited 10 billion as a milestone number, meaning Tesla is within months of hitting it given current daily mileage accumulation rates. Brian argued the significance is less about any specific threshold and more about the continuous improvement in training data output.

7. Tesla's FSD version 14.2.2.5 was highlighted for navigating a large highway flood puddle spanning nearly two lanes. The panel contrasted this with Waymo, which stops functioning in heavy rain or snow due to LiDAR scatter — a point Elon Musk himself made publicly. The panel noted Waymo's accident rate has remained flat quarter-over-quarter rather than improving, and criticized the broader legacy autonomous vehicle approach of retrofitting off-the-shelf vehicles with expensive sensor arrays rather than designing purpose-built hardware.

8. Tesla Giga New York (the original SolarCity Buffalo factory) was confirmed job-compliant with its $1/year lease agreement with New York State. The facility received $350 million in state funding and a nominal lease in exchange for job creation targets. After Tesla wound down its auto-labeling team there, compliance was in question. The factory now manufactures Superchargers (and potentially Megachargers) and is ramping solar panel production again, with Powerwalls manufactured in Nevada. A service center in Long Island was also counted toward the statewide job threshold.

9. The Boring Company's Nashville tunnel was approved unanimously by city council after a public hearing where officials asked basic questions about EV fire safety and wheel integrity. The panel noted that Boring's proof rock boring machine can drill through any terrain — in Las Vegas they simultaneously pumped groundwater while drilling. The tunnel lining system is a 12–14 inch six-piece overlapping ring designed for global terrain variability. The project is entirely privately funded at no cost to taxpayers. John noted that of the three tunneling companies operating in the US, one was acquired by a Chinese firm and another went bankrupt, leaving the Boring Company as the primary domestic option.

10. Additional brief items covered: the Model 3 won Edmunds' top-rated electric vehicle award; the Model S yoke steering wheel option was quietly removed from the configurator (except on the Plaid); GM and Ford were criticized for continued mixed signals on EV commitment while writing down tens of billions in EV losses; Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund-backed AI company Humane took a significant stake in xAI prior to the SpaceX-xAI merger discussions; and the Napa, California police department acquired a Tesla for DUI enforcement.

Notable quotes and data: "This is not a step you take when a product is two years from release. This is something you do when release is imminent." — Brian, on Cybercab production starting. "If you look at Waymo's safety report quarter by quarter, you'll find that their accident rate is steady. It is not improving." — Brian. Tesla stock at time of recording: $410, down approximately 6% year-to-date. Tesla Energy deployed an all-time high of 46.7 gigawatt hours. F-150 Raptor price: $47,000 in 2019 vs. $79,000 MSRP in 2026. Original 2019 Cybertruck AWD announcement price of $49,900 equals approximately $63,000 in inflation-adjusted 2026 dollars.

2026-02-21.title: Why Tesla Robotaxis Are CLOSE To Inflection - And So Is The Stock
2026-02-21.body:
The video analyzes Tesla's robotaxi deployment trajectory as a leading indicator for the stock's next major move. The presenter, using a "robotaxi tracker" tool, argues that fleet growth data reveals Tesla is in an endgame phase of collecting regulatory data for driverless approval in California. His central thesis is that the robotaxi fleet growth curve and the declining stock price are fundamentally incompatible and will converge — creating a significant stock catalyst — around April 2026.

Key Points:

1. Current fleet size and trajectory. As of the video, Tesla has 386 total robotaxis deployed across Austin, Texas and the Bay Area, California. The fleet started with just 11 units in Austin on June 24th (2025) and 3 in the Bay Area around September 1st, making the current scale a meaningful increase from a very small base.

2. Operational differences between Austin and Bay Area. In Austin, Tesla deploys safety drivers in the passenger seat — not legally required in Texas, but a voluntary Tesla safety measure that can be removed at Tesla's discretion. In California's Bay Area, drivers must sit behind the wheel, and California regulations require a minimum threshold of supervised miles to be submitted to regulators before driverless operation is permitted.

3. The December inflection point in fleet data. Around December 30th, Tesla dramatically changed its deployment pace, growing the Bay Area fleet from 153 to 295 vehicles — nearly doubling in 6 to 7 weeks. The presenter interprets this as Tesla deliberately starting the official data collection window it intends to submit to California regulators, since operators can choose their starting point for the mileage count but cannot manipulate data after that point begins.

4. Tesla is in the endgame for California driverless permits. The fleet doubling strategy is consistent with wanting to accumulate the required 50,000 to 60,000 supervised miles as quickly as possible to submit for a driverless operating permit. The presenter believes Tesla is very close to receiving California driverless approval and speculates it could be announced around April 2026, which he notes could itself serve as a minor stock catalyst.

5. Unsupervised fleet in Texas is still tiny but about to expand rapidly. Only 7 vehicles in Austin are currently operating truly unsupervised. The presenter predicts this number will hockey-stick in April, driven by the launch of the Cybercab — a two-seat vehicle that physically cannot accommodate a passenger-seat safety monitor. This creates a forcing function: Tesla must have unsupervised operation approved and operational in Austin before Cybercab can carry paying passengers at all.

6. The 1,800 robotaxi prediction and its financial significance. The presenter made a prediction in June 2025 that Tesla would reach 1,800 fully commercial robotaxis by April 2026. He argues this threshold matters because it would generate approximately 5% of Tesla's 2024 annual free cash flow on an annualized basis — enough to be a meaningful, measurable driver of cash flow that would show up materially in Q2 2026 earnings.

7. The stock-versus-fleet divergence is the core alpha opportunity. The Tesla stock price has been sliding downward since approximately December 19th, following a pattern similar to the prior year's post-peak drift. Meanwhile, the robotaxi fleet count has been climbing sharply. The presenter argues these two trend lines are fundamentally incompatible over the medium term and will have to converge — either the stock stops declining or it begins rising as the fleet growth becomes impossible for the market to ignore.

8. Near-term stock trajectory and reversal timing. The presenter expects the stock to continue drifting toward the $400 level or possibly lower in the near term, with the slide reversing when the fleet roughly doubles again from current levels to somewhere around 600 to 800 vehicles. He estimates this reversal will occur within 2 to 3 weeks of the video's recording, with the larger hockey-stick move happening at the 1,800 vehicle inflection point in April.

9. Macro risks acknowledged. The presenter notes that broader macro conditions — specifically referencing tensions involving Iran — could introduce additional pressure on the stock in March and potentially disrupt the timing of this thesis, even if the underlying robotaxi story remains intact.

Conclusions and Recommendations:
The presenter frames the robotaxi fleet count as the single most important variable to watch for Tesla stock positioning. He suggests this data offers an alpha opportunity for options traders and tactical stock traders because the market has not yet repriced the stock to reflect the accelerating fleet deployment. His recommended mental model is to watch for fleet milestone numbers — 600, 900, and ultimately 1,800 — as the forcing functions that will drive stock price reversals.

Notable Quotes and Data:
- Fleet growth from 153 to 295 Bay Area vehicles in approximately 6-7 weeks starting December 30th — roughly a 93% increase.
- Only 7 truly unsupervised robotaxis currently operating in Texas.
- 89 supervised vehicles currently in Austin, for a combined Austin total near 96.
- Regulatory mileage threshold for California driverless permit estimated at 50,000 to 60,000 miles.
- 1,800 commercial robotaxis projected to equal approximately 5% of Tesla's 2024 annualized free cash flow.
- "These two lines are incompatible in the long run with each other."
- "Once that happens, it's absolutely game over."

2026-02-22.title: Elon Just Showed Us His Entire Playbook
2026-02-22.body:
The video analyzes four specific posts Elon Musk made on X within a single week in early March 2026, arguing they collectively reveal a coherent strategic roadmap for Tesla's transformation from an automaker into an autonomy company. The host, who runs the channel brighterwithherbert, frames these posts not as random social media activity but as deliberate signals about Tesla's direction in robotics, autonomous vehicles, and AI. The central thesis is that every post pointed toward a single theme: autonomy is accelerating, and Tesla is the center of that acceleration.

1. Optimus timeline correction (Friday post). Cathie Wood of Ark Invest posted that Tesla's Optimus robot would begin transforming factory and home life in 2028-2029, noting humanoid robot development is 200,000 times more complex than robo-taxis. Elon replied directly, pushing the timeline earlier: transformation begins in 2027, becomes obvious in 2028, and reaches massive impact by 2029. The host emphasizes that publicly correcting one of Tesla's most prominent bulls — and accelerating her already-bullish timeline by a year — is not something done casually. It represents a firm public commitment, not a casual boast.

2. Autonomous fleet declaration (Saturday post). Elon posted that Tesla will have "the largest fleet of autonomous vehicles as far into the future as I can imagine," using declarative language ("will have," not "might have"). For context, Waymo currently operates roughly 3,000 robo-taxis across six U.S. cities after more than a decade of operation and billions in Google funding. Tesla has approximately 383 robo-taxis — 89 in Austin and 294 in the Bay Area — but only launched the service about eight months prior. The host also notes a significant revelation about Waymo this same week: Waymo confirmed it maintains roughly 70 remote operators on duty at any given time, with half based in the Philippines and half in the U.S. The host argues this shows Waymo still depends on human infrastructure, while Tesla is pursuing true edge autonomy with no remote operators, which it claims will allow superior long-term scaling.

3. Grok 4.2 announcement (same weekend). Elon announced that Grok 4.2 was "finally" shipping the following week, describing it as a significant improvement over 4.1. The host acknowledges this is technically an XAI announcement, not a Tesla one, but argues the two companies are increasingly intertwined. SpaceX and XAI merged earlier in March 2026. The Colossus data center in Nashville — described as the world's largest AI training cluster — benefits both companies, and neural network expertise flows between Tesla's FSD development and XAI's Grok research. The word "finally" is highlighted as suggesting prior delays, but the fact that it is now shipping signals momentum. Additionally, Tesla's own Cortex 1 and Cortex 2 data centers are in development, with Colossus 2 projected to reach 1 million H100 equivalents.

4. First Cybercab production unit and $30,000 pricing confirmation (Tuesday post). Elon congratulated the Tesla team on the first production Cybercab rolling off the line at Giga Texas — not a prototype, but an actual production unit with no steering wheel and no pedals, designed from the ground up for full autonomy. He called this historic. The host connects this to a public bet: tech reviewer MKBHD (Marques Brownlee) had challenged in 2024 that Tesla would never pull this off and pledged to shave his head on camera if wrong. When someone on X asked Elon directly whether Tesla would sell a Cybercab for $30,000 or less before 2027, he replied yes. The host treats this as a particularly strong commitment because Elon was responding to a bet challenge, not just a neutral question, suggesting he has genuine confidence in the timeline.

The host's overarching interpretation is that Tesla is executing a coherent technology stack: FSD generates training data, robo-taxi service scales the software, Cybercab is the dedicated hardware platform, Optimus runs on the same AI stack, and Grok advances underlying neural net capabilities. Each piece reinforces the others. Nothing posted that week was about car sales, margins, or traditional automotive competition.

The host addresses three predictable objections. On cherry-picking: the other posts that week were about politics and memes, and these four were the only substantive Tesla-relevant ones. On missed timelines: Elon's track record on near-term promises is poor — robo-taxi by 2020 never happened, FSD "next year" was promised repeatedly — but the host argues the present situation is different because hardware is actually shipping and robo-taxis are carrying real passengers. On Grok not being Tesla: the SpaceX-XAI merger made the ecosystem integration explicit.

The host also offers a nuanced read on Elon's forecasting accuracy: he tends to be wrong on one-year timelines, more accurate on very short-term (weeks/months) commitments, and generally correct on long-horizon technology predictions.

Calls to action and milestones to watch: Mark April 2026 for Cybercab mass production start at Giga Texas (drone footage already shows crash-testing units exiting the factory). Watch for Optimus Gen 3 reveal this quarter — described as the mass-production-ready design, with Fremont production lines being built toward a 1 million units per year run rate. Track Grok 4.2 benchmarks when it ships. Monitor whether a $30,000 Cybercab is sold to a consumer before 2027 as the clearest validation of the entire timeline.

Notable quotes or data: Elon on Optimus: "It will begin to transform things in 2027. It'll be obvious in 2028 and have a massive impact by 2029." Elon on fleet size: "Tesla will have the largest fleet of autonomous vehicles as far into the future as I can imagine." Waymo's remote operator count: approximately 70 on duty at any time, split between the Philippines and the U.S. Tesla's current robo-taxi count: 383 vehicles (89 Austin, 294 Bay Area), launched roughly 8 months prior versus Waymo's 3,000 over a decade. Cybercab confirmed price point: $30,000 or less before 2027. Elon has stated he aims to be accurate 50% of the time — meaning roughly half his predictions run ahead of schedule and half behind.

2026-02-22.title: Tesla’s Robotaxi Timeline Just Accelerated
2026-02-22.body:
The video features host Herbert (of brighterwithherbert) and guest Larry Goldberg, a serial entrepreneur and venture capital investor, discussing newly released Tesla FSD safety data and NHTSA incident reports for Tesla's robotaxi service. The central thesis is that Tesla's autonomous driving program is on a clear and accelerating trajectory toward fully unsupervised operation, with safety metrics already outpacing human drivers and no meaningful obstacles to a broader rollout. The conversation also covers Tesla's competitive pricing advantage over Uber and Waymo, and the broader public awareness gap around FSD capabilities.

1. Tesla has now accumulated 8.2 billion FSD supervised miles, with 10 billion miles cited as Elon Musk's threshold for unsupervised self-driving. The counter jumped 500 million miles in a single day due to recalibrated usage tracking. Analyst "Cern" projects the 10 billion mile mark will be reached by June 22, approximately four months from the recording date of early March 2026. Both hosts note Musk likely intended this as a rough approximation rather than a precise cutoff.

2. Tesla's supervised FSD is already dramatically safer than human driving by every major metric. The data shows 7 times fewer major collisions, 7 times fewer minor collisions, and 5 times fewer off-highway collisions compared to average U.S. driving. A Tesla running FSD supervised travels 5.3 million miles before a major collision, versus 660,000 miles for the U.S. average. The hosts argue this supervised safety record is a strong predictor of unsupervised performance.

3. Tesla's Austin robotaxi fleet has logged 14 total NHTSA-reported incidents since launching in June 2025, across 225,000 miles driven in the final two months of 2025. The five most recent incidents (December–January) all occurred at extremely low speeds: 0, 1, 2, 4, and 17 mph. There were zero hospitalizations, zero airbag deployments, and no towing required. One incident was not Tesla's fault at all; two were minor parking lot contacts. NHTSA mandates that all incidents be reported and categorizes them as "crashes" regardless of severity, which the hosts say is being exploited by outlets like Bloomberg for misleading headlines.

4. FSD supervised usage is skyrocketing, with Tesla owners averaging more than 20 million miles per day in Q1 2026, a new all-time high representing a 30% increase from just a few months prior. This translates to over 13,000 miles per minute. Both hosts attribute this surge to the quality of FSD version 14, which has become smooth enough that even skeptical passengers no longer object to it being engaged.

5. Larry Goldberg rode both the Tesla robotaxi and Waymo during a recent Austin visit and found the Tesla dramatically smoother. He described the Waymo experience as physically uncomfortable — jittery around corners, inconsistent braking — while the Tesla was smooth enough that on a prior visit he forgot he was in a robotaxi mid-conversation. This echoes Tesla community analyst Omar's observation that riding Waymo now feels like going back to FSD version 10 or 11.

6. Goldberg's Uber receipts from the Austin trip illustrate the pricing vulnerability Tesla will exploit. Over 32 miles of Uber rides, he paid $177.83 — a cost per mile of $5.44. A 3.2-mile round trip to a barbecue restaurant during peak hours cost $84.94. He argues Uber's entire profit model depends on peak-hour and premium surges above $4 per mile, and Tesla will undercut this substantially. His estimate is Tesla will price robotaxi rides at roughly $2 per mile or less — less than half of comparable Uber pricing — while Tesla's internal cost target is reportedly 25–50 cents per mile.

7. Tesla's structural advantage over both Uber and Waymo in peak demand is the ability to dynamically pull from its retail owner fleet. When demand spikes, Tesla can offer owners real-time incentives (e.g., $2/mile) to dispatch their personal vehicles autonomously. The car would go to a Tesla facility for cleaning and inspection, complete rides, and return to the owner's home by morning. Waymo cannot scale elastically this way, and Uber depends on human drivers voluntarily coming online for surge pay.

8. Larry Goldberg predicts that by mid-2026, FSD unsupervised will be demonstrably safer than any human driver, and the robotaxi fleet will be expanding at a "dizzying rate." He adds that the Model Y and Model 3 are essentially already there on safety, while the Cybertruck still needs work. He anticipates the full fleet being "dialed in" by end of June 2026.

9. Both hosts argue Tesla urgently needs to advertise FSD capabilities to a mainstream audience. Goldberg recounted lunch guests — educated, attentive people — who were completely unaware of FSD's current capabilities. He and the host suggest Tesla's existing video content and community-created footage could form highly effective ads, but Tesla currently limits distribution to X, which they argue only reaches already-converted followers.

Notable quotes or data:
- "7 times fewer major collisions, 7 times fewer minor collisions, 5 times fewer off-highway collisions" — Tesla FSD supervised vs. average U.S. driving
- "5.3 million miles before a major collision" with FSD supervised vs. 660,000 miles for the U.S. average
- "$5.44 per mile" — Goldberg's effective Uber cost per mile in Austin over 32 miles, $177.83 total
- "$84.94 for a 3.2-mile round trip" to Terry Black's BBQ during peak hours
- "An average of 110 people die every day" on U.S. roads from human driving
- "32,000 lives could already be saved per year if supervised FSD were universally available"
- 20 million FSD miles per day in Q1 2026, up 30% in a few months; 13,000+ miles per minute

2026-02-22.title: Tesla's Lessons Echo through China - XPeng Follows the Lead
2026-02-22.body:
Brian, host of the Futuraza YouTube channel, visited Guangzhou, China to attend an XPeng (Xiaopeng) Motors product event and factory tour. The video covers his firsthand observations of XPeng's manufacturing capabilities, their new product announcements, and a direct Q&A exchange with XPeng's CEO. His central thesis is that XPeng has meaningfully adopted Tesla's manufacturing philosophy while carving out its own path — representing a genuine competitive force, particularly against legacy automakers.

Key Points:

1. XPeng is introducing Extended Range EVs (EREVs) for northern China markets. Originally a pure electric vehicle company, XPeng made the strategic decision to add a combustion engine to some models. Brian explains the reasoning: in northern China, temperatures compromise battery range, and very few consumers have access to home charging. He distinguishes EREVs from plug-in hybrids by framing it clearly — a plug-in hybrid is essentially a gas car with an electric motor added, while an EREV is fundamentally an electric car that uses a gas engine as a range extender, yielding significantly more electric range.

2. XPeng's factory uses giga castings and a structural battery pack, directly mirroring Tesla's approach. Brian notes that XPeng's front and rear castings are actually larger than those Tesla uses. He frames this as deliberate adoption of proven Tesla manufacturing innovations rather than coincidence. However, he also notes XPeng's software is meaningfully different, and the UI similarity across Chinese EV brands is simply a function of limited ways to present vehicle information clearly.

3. The assembly line uses a sophisticated dual-robot system for panoramic roof glass installation. One robot laser-scans the exact surface of each individual vehicle, while a second robot applies a precisely calculated amount of adhesive based on that scan. This addresses a historic weak point in automotive manufacturing — panoramic sunroof leaks — by compensating in real time for any stamping irregularities unique to that specific car. Brian had a robotics expert (Jake, formerly of Faraday Future) review the footage, who confirmed the robots are standard ABB units and the line looks entirely conventional and professional.

4. Brian asked XPeng's CEO a pointed question about Full Self-Driving performance, referencing David Moss's 11,000-mile U.S. drive with zero disengagements. He used this real-world Tesla FSD benchmark to press the CEO on how XPeng's XNGP (referred to as "Eli 2.0" and "BLA 2.0" in context) would compare. The CEO's response was candid: city driving will be sorted out far sooner than rural or cross-country driving, and achieving coast-to-coast (or full-width-of-China) autonomous capability could take up to 5 years due to poor road infrastructure and greater hazards outside major cities. Brian called this "a very bold admission."

5. XPeng holds a robotaxi permit in Guangzhou and plans to begin operating robotaxis by August 2025, with a confident end-of-year target. They have not yet begun commercial robotaxi operations as of the time of filming, but the permit is already secured. This places them in direct competition with other Chinese autonomous vehicle players racing toward commercialization.

6. The event celebrated the 100,000th unit of a specific model rolling off the Guangzhou assembly line. Brian was skeptical in the moment about whether the milestone was real or ceremonial marketing, but attended the full CEO presentation, which included real-time English translation via an earpiece fed by a bilingual employee standing at the back of the room with a transmitter — a detail Brian found notable and charming.

7. XPeng's influencer marketing culture was on prominent display at the product event. Brian described a large semicircle of influencers simultaneously reading from their phones while presenting the car — a sharp contrast to Western automotive press events and a reflection of how Chinese EV brands approach consumer-facing promotion.

Conclusions and Recommendations:

Brian's overall assessment is that XPeng is a serious manufacturer that has absorbed the right lessons from Tesla's production innovations without simply copying everything. He sees China's EV sector as holding real advantages over legacy Western automakers, though not necessarily over Tesla itself. He encourages viewers to engage critically in the comments and flags that more factory footage is forthcoming. He also promotes a potential small-group factory tour he was organizing for March 6th or 7th in Guangzhou for viewers in the area.

Notable Quotes and Data:

"China's got some advantages, not necessarily over Tesla, but certainly over a lot of the legacy car makers."

David Moss drove 11,000 miles in the US on Tesla FSD with zero disengagements — the benchmark Brian put directly to XPeng's CEO.

XPeng's CEO estimated it could take 5 years to achieve full-width-of-China autonomous driving capability outside cities.

XPeng targets robotaxi operations in Guangzhou by August, with end-of-2025 as the outer bound.

XPeng's giga castings are described as larger than Tesla's.

2026-02-22.title: Why Tesla Cybercab Is ON TRACK to BOOST The Stock
2026-02-22.body:
Tesla's Cybercab production line has officially come online, and the speaker frames this as a historic inflection point for both the company and human transportation broadly. The central thesis is that Wall Street is underestimating the pace and scale of Cybercab deployment, creating a significant investment opportunity for those paying close attention. The speaker, who identifies as a Tesla investor and analyst rather than a car enthusiast, argues that the Cybercab's existence as a purpose-built robotaxi with no steering wheel or pedals makes its intended use case undeniable — and that the market has not yet priced this in correctly.

Key Points:

1. First production Cybercabs spotted in Austin, Texas. A community drone operator and Tesla observer known as "Joe Techmire" captured footage of the first serially produced Cybercabs emerging from the factory. The vehicles are identifiable as production units by the stickers on the windows, though they were missing wheel covers at the time of spotting. The speaker treats this as a landmark moment — the first tangible evidence that Cybercab has moved from prototype to actual manufacturing output.

2. The final production line is complete and live. Tesla confirmed that the first Cybercab produced on the final, completed production line has rolled off. The speaker references commentary from supply chain and production expert Jeff Lutz, who appeared on the channel and described this as the "decisive moment" — meaning the manufacturing infrastructure is no longer in setup mode but is operational and ready to scale.

3. The "unboxed" manufacturing system could produce up to 5 million units per year. The speaker describes Tesla's revolutionary unboxed production method as capable of producing one vehicle every six seconds, compared to a conventional rate of one every 30 seconds — roughly a 5x improvement in throughput. This single production line, he claims, could theoretically output around 5 million Cybercabs annually, which he argues is the volume that will ultimately be needed.

4. The ramp-up trajectory runs from now through April 2026. The speaker outlines a stepped production increase: a few units per day currently, scaling to tens, then hundreds per month through March, with the full ramp beginning in April. He notes that the first several hundred units will be used for crash testing, quality inspection, gap and adhesive analysis, and disassembly — standard validation processes — with some also deployed for real-world testing to fine-tune the production feedback loop.

5. The Cybercab has only one possible purpose: unsupervised robotaxi service. Because the vehicle has no steering wheel, no pedals, and no side mirrors, the speaker argues there is literally no other use case for it. This is not a consumer car that could be sold to individuals as a personal vehicle — it must function as an autonomous robotaxi. He positions this as the key insight the market is missing, framing it as a clash between skeptical Wall Street analysts and the undeniable physical reality of what Tesla is building.

6. The market is pricing in a much slower rollout than what Tesla is signaling. The speaker contends that Wall Street's current valuation assumes perhaps 1,000 Cybercabs operational by December 2026, whereas the physical evidence — billions invested, a fully live production line, an entire factory workforce dedicated to this — suggests tens of thousands of units, potentially 20,000 to 30,000, could be deployed by year-end. If the higher figure is correct, he argues the stock would move dramatically upward.

7. Tesla's investment commitment itself signals confidence. The speaker makes a logical argument: Tesla has spent billions of dollars, vast management attention, and enormous human resources building a dedicated manufacturing line for a vehicle that can only serve one purpose. This level of commitment, he says, would be irrational unless Tesla's leadership was certain that autonomous, unsupervised robotaxi operation was achievable at scale in the near term.

8. A brief question is raised about regulatory risk. A viewer named Shane posed a question in the speaker's community platform about whether the Self-Drive Act and new federal regulations could slow Tesla's scaling ability. The speaker acknowledges this is a legitimate and interesting question he plans to address in a future video, but does not answer it in this video.

Conclusions and Recommendations: The speaker's core recommendation is implicitly investment-oriented — he frames the gap between Wall Street's expectations and Tesla's actual production progress as "alpha," meaning an opportunity to outperform the market by recognizing something others have not yet priced in. He states that from now through mid-April 2026, the Cybercab and robotaxi rollout should be the primary focus for Tesla investors, above other catalysts like Optimus or SpaceAI. He also promotes his Pioneer Alpha investment community at pioneeralpha.org, noting it is now available to users in the EU, Canada, the UK, and Switzerland in addition to Americans.

Notable quotes or data: "This is not a car. This is a robot on wheels." — "The vision is eating reality because the vision is now reality." — "If you're right and they have 20 or 30,000 [robotaxis by December], the stock will go to the moon." — Production capacity claim: one vehicle every 6 seconds versus a conventional rate of one every 30 seconds, representing a roughly 5x improvement. — Potential annual output cited: 5 million Cybercabs from a single production line. — Wireless charging efficiency statistic referenced in passing from the community platform: 93%.

2026-02-23.title: Tesla and Elon Musk Just Dropped New Announcements
2026-02-23.body:
This episode of Brighterwithherbert covers a cluster of Tesla and Elon Musk announcements from the weekend, spanning CyberCab production progress, Full Self-Driving software upgrades, robotaxi fleet growth, hiring expansion, and a surprise Cybertruck pricing move. Host Herbert is joined by Jeff Lutz, a multi-decade supply chain executive and former chief quality officer who now runs a high-tech manufacturing consulting firm. The discussion blends news reporting with supply chain and operational analysis.

1. CyberCab production vehicles spotted at Giga Texas. Drone footage captured by Joe Techmire showed three CyberCabs outside the Giga Texas facility, confirmed as production versions based on tire stickers and wheel designs matching those used in the production area. The vehicles had no steering wheels and no side mirrors, consistent with the final production configuration, and were observed near the crash testing facility, suggesting they are being prepared for structural and reliability validation ahead of the April start of production.

2. The pre-SOP manufacturing process explained. Jeff Lutz described the current phase as a convergence period between first-off-production and start of production, during which small "steel safe" changes are made to supplier tooling and factory integration simultaneously. These changes are batched into "recipe" builds — a small number of cars assembled under a defined set of modifications — which are then run through testing to chip away at remaining issues before the line rate is formally increased at SOP.

3. Voice commands coming to FSD; Grok integration underway. Elon Musk confirmed that FSD will soon accept natural language voice commands, allowing drivers to say things like "find a parking spot near the door" or "turn right at the next block" rather than using manual inputs. Jeff framed this as Tesla potentially being the first automaker to deploy a real large language model integrated into vehicle controls, with Grok already partially integrated; he expects Tesla's implementation to have lower latency and tighter safety guardrails than competitors, preventing commands that would cause unsafe maneuvers at speed.

4. FSD Supervised already recognizes hand gestures. Both Tesla Europe and Elon Musk confirmed that FSD Supervised responds to hand gestures from people outside the vehicle. A video from the Netherlands showed a CyberCab-era Tesla squeezing through a tight lane as the car responded to a person's hand signal. Jeff noted he has personally observed the system responding to police officers directing traffic, with the car waiting and then proceeding when the officer signals.

5. Robotaxi fleet at 395 vehicles, 8 unsupervised. The total fleet grew from roughly 390 to 395 over just a few days, with 306 cars operating in the Bay Area and 89 in Austin. The subset of fully unsupervised vehicles grew from 7 to 8, representing 18% of the 44-vehicle active fleet. Jeff noted that for Tesla to meet Elon's earnings call commitment to roughly double the fleet by end of quarter, they would need to reach just over 500 total — meaning a significant push is still required this week.

6. Why fleet growth is deliberately slow. Jeff explained that Tesla's internal constraint is its own probabilistic safety model: as fleet density increases, the probability of an incident rises unless software capability improves faster than the density. The team is actively crushing edge-case bugs before expanding further. A secondary constraint is operational capacity — charging logistics, vehicle cleaning, and service infrastructure all need to scale in parallel with the number of cars.

7. Misleading January crash headlines flagged as a reputational risk. Five low-speed incidents in January — all under 4 miles per hour and all parking-related — were reported by media as crashes, with some outlets characterizing Tesla as four times more dangerous than human drivers. Herbert and Jeff argued these were minor contact events, not meaningful safety failures, but expressed concern that the narrative is spreading and could deter riders from using the service. Both called on Tesla's PR team to respond more aggressively, noting that silence risks the misleading headlines becoming accepted public knowledge.

8. Hiring now spans 14 cities. Tesla posted a new Robotaxi Operations Manager role in Tampa, Florida, and additional AI Safety Operator positions in Vienna, Virginia, bringing the total number of cities where these roles are being filled to 14, up from 12 the prior week. Cities now include locations like Draper, Utah and Peabody, Massachusetts that had not previously been discussed. Jeff noted that many of these roles are full-time, suggesting employees will be transitioned into other service functions once the supervised phase gives way to fully unsupervised operations.

9. New Cybertruck trim at $59,990 offered for 10 days only. Tesla launched a lower-cost Cybertruck configuration at $59,990, with 325 miles of range, 7,500-pound towing capacity, and rear-wheel steering, but with notable deletions including smaller wheels, no rear screen, no vault storage compartment, and cloth seats. Elon stated the price is only available for 10 days and that continuation depends on demand levels observed during the window.

10. Jeff's supply chain read on the Cybertruck pricing. Jeff interpreted the 10-day window as likely serving two simultaneous purposes: clearing upstream piece-part inventory (what he called excess and obsolescence management) tied to supplier commitments, and price discovery to determine whether meaningful demand exists at the $60K level. If demand proves strong and sustained, Tesla could recalculate factory fixed-cost absorption and decide to make the price permanent. If not, Jeff speculated Tesla may redirect Cybertruck production toward an autonomous delivery or "robo-truck" use case, given Cybertruck's prior underperformance against sales forecasts at higher price points.

Notable quotes and data:
— "85% of car trips in North America have just one or two people" — cited by Sawyer Merritt to explain the CyberCab's two-seat design.
— Average vehicle occupancy per trip for all U.S. household vehicles: 1.5 people, per Jeff.
— Electronics industry excess and obsolescence benchmark: approximately 0.3% of average sale price.
— The $50,000 dual-motor Cybertruck unveiled in 2019 is equivalent to roughly $63,000 in today's dollars when adjusted for inflation — making the new $59,990 version described as fair market value.
— Jeff: "Tesla at $60,000 for everything that it does as an owner is an amazing value."
— Elon, quoted on FSD performance: "It only gets better from here."
— Elon on voice commands: confirmed they are "coming."
— Jeff on LLM vehicle integration: "It's what Siri could have been."

2026-02-23.title: The Robotaxi War Is Really About Scale
2026-02-23.body:
The robotaxi competitive landscape is fundamentally a question of infrastructure and scale, not just current vehicle counts. Host Herbert and guest Larry Goldberg, a serial entrepreneur and venture capital investor, argue that the ability to support tens of thousands of autonomous vehicles — through charging, servicing, and logistics — is the decisive factor separating viable operators from those who will struggle. ARK Invest research forms the backbone of the discussion, revealing a robotaxi total addressable market far larger than conventional estimates suggest.

Key Points:

1. Infrastructure is the decisive competitive moat, not fleet size. Waymo has announced 3,000 robotaxis, Zoox has a few hundred, and Tesla has several hundred — but the number of vehicles is a misleading metric. What matters is who can support tens of thousands of vehicles with charging, servicing, and cleaning at scale. Waymo, for instance, must park vehicles in public lots where they are vulnerable to damage, subcontracts charging infrastructure, and relies on third-party partners for maintenance — all of which adds cost and friction.

2. Tesla received an FCC waiver for ultra-wideband wireless EV charging positioning technology. The FCC granted Tesla an exception to standard rules that prohibit ultra-wideband radio from being installed outdoors on fixed equipment. The system uses a radio transceiver in the vehicle and one embedded in a ground-level charging pad to guide the Cybercab into a precise position over the charger — critical for maximizing charging rate and minimizing time on the charger. The signal is low-power, activates only briefly while parked, and operates at very short range, which satisfied FCC interference concerns. Larry speculates this regulatory delay may have been one reason wireless charging rollout has been slow.

3. Wireless charging precision is a fleet operations requirement, not a convenience feature. Unlike consumer phones where imprecise pad placement only adds a few minutes, a robotaxi fleet requires exact positioning to achieve maximum charge rates and rapid vehicle turnover so the next car can immediately take the charger's spot. This automation of the charging process — removing any human intervention — is described as a "game changer" for fleet operations, particularly during peak demand hours when surge capacity is critical.

4. Tesla's infrastructure advantage is structural and multi-layered. Tesla already has service centers in both major and minor cities across the United States, the world's largest supercharging network, and purpose-built vehicles designed for minimal servicing. Larry argues Tesla actually needs less infrastructure than competitors because its vehicles are more self-contained. Adding wireless charging pads to existing supercharger stations is expected to be relatively straightforward, leveraging the same deployment capability that built out the supercharger network at a pace nobody thought possible.

5. Uber's $100 million investment in autonomous charging hubs signals it is in catch-up mode and faces structural challenges. Uber announced it will build DC fast charging stations at its autonomous depots and pit stops throughout cities, partnering with more than 20 charging operators including EVgo (New York, Los Angeles, San Francisco, Boston), Electra (Paris, Madrid), and Ionity (London). Larry's critique: at even $50,000 per charging station, $100 million only buys roughly 2,000 units. More critically, Uber's existing business relies on owner-drivers for roughly 80% or more of its logistics — a model that disappears entirely in full autonomy. Reinventing that entire operational stack through 50 different partners is viewed as unworkable.

6. ARK Invest research pegs the US robotaxi market at $3.9 trillion annually at equilibrium, or $2.8 trillion even if users never give up their personal vehicles. ARK analyst Brett Winton's work shows that at $3 per mile (roughly current Uber pricing), the total addressable market is under $500 billion. As price per mile drops to $1.50, the TAM reaches approximately $1 trillion. At $0.50 per mile, the TAM approaches $3 trillion. ARK's research indicates Tesla can earn a solid profit at $0.50 per mile, creating a 6x market expansion versus today's ride-hail pricing.

7. The market opportunity extends beyond ride-hail into "substitution value" — the economic cost of time spent driving. Americans spend $1.6 trillion annually on direct driving costs, but ARK calculates they devote an additional $4.4 trillion worth of productive labor time to the manual task of piloting vehicles — a total effective cost of approximately $6 trillion per year. This time cost is currently invisible in GDP accounting. As robotaxi prices fall to $2 per mile or below, people who already own cars will increasingly choose to be driven rather than drive, not to save money on transport but to recapture productive work time.

8. High-income earners are the primary target market and paradoxically are the heaviest manual drivers. ARK's income decile analysis shows that the wealthiest workers — executives, doctors, professionals — both do the most driving and have the highest willingness to pay for robotaxi service because their time carries the greatest economic value. Larry notes that currently only the very wealthy hire drivers, but robotaxi could democratize this from middle management upward. The willingness-to-pay clearing price scales from fractions of a cent per mile at the lowest income decile to roughly $2.40 per mile at the top decile.

9. Macroeconomic implications are significant but currently unrecognized. The $4.3 trillion in labor cost absorbed by manual driving is invisible to GDP accounting. Freeing that time for productive work would add measurably to national economic output. The hosts note that New York's governor has moved to pause robotaxi rollout, likely due to pressure from taxi driver unions, and argue that politicians will need to weigh this productivity data against labor concerns.

Conclusions and recommendations: The path to transforming transportation runs through price reduction, which requires scale, which requires purpose-built vehicles and fully owned infrastructure. At $2 per mile or below, the robotaxi market expands dramatically. Tesla's Cybercab, supercharger network, service center footprint, and now FCC-approved wireless positioning technology are presented as a compounding structural advantage that competitors cannot easily replicate. The hosts recommend following Larry Goldberg at lumisanti.com and Herbert's Tesla investor resource site at herbalm.com.

Notable quotes and data:
- US robotaxi TAM at equilibrium: $3.9 trillion annually; $2.8 trillion even without car ownership changes
- Americans spend $1.6 trillion annually on direct driving costs; $4.4 trillion in forfeited labor time — total $6 trillion effective cost
- Uber's average revenue: approximately $4 per mile; profit is concentrated in the $8+ per mile rides
- ARK finds Tesla can profit at $0.50 per mile
- TAM at $3/mile: under $500 billion; at $0.50/mile: approximately $3 trillion — a 6x expansion
- Uber's $100 million charging investment: at $50,000 per station, yields roughly 2,000 charging units
- Larry: "In autonomy there are no owner drivers. There's an entire world that has to be reinvented."
- On Uber's 50-partner strategy: "You do not reinvent things with 50 different partners."

2026-02-23.title: INSANE used prices, Cybertrucks on Screen, MKBHD Beef & MORE
2026-02-23.body:
This is a Monday news roundup from Brian at the Futuraza channel, covering a range of EV and Tesla-related headlines from the previous week. Brian's tone is conversational and editorially opinionated, frequently pushing back on what he considers poorly researched or biased journalism. The video covers Tesla Model Y L global expansion, used EV pricing trends, MKBHD's relationship with Tesla, Uber's robotaxi ambitions, Ford's factory closure, and California's EV semi voucher controversy, ending with a live Q&A session.

Key Points:

1. Tesla Model Y L (longer wheelbase, six-seat variant) is expanding globally after Australia certification. Tesla launched the YL in China in August 2025, where it was well-received. It has now been officially listed in Australia's automotive regulator database under the designation Y L5NDB, serviced by the Shanghai factory. Brian personally reviewed the car in China the prior month and noted the 2+2 configuration is more comfortable than the standard 7-seat Model Y layout. A European launch also seems likely given that the YL was spotted testing at the Nurburgring and seen at Giga Berlin. Brian believes a US launch from the Texas factory is coming by late 2026, and hints at having some basis for that confidence beyond public information.

2. Tesla used car prices are rising while other EVs depreciate sharply. Brian debunks the persistent narrative that Tesla has terrible resale value, tracing it to the pandemic-era pricing distortion when dealers were marking up vehicles like a $35,000 Toyota to $40,000-$45,000 over MSRP. When the $7,500 federal EV tax credit disappeared, it lifted the floor on used Tesla prices because new Teslas became comparatively more expensive, while other EVs that previously benefited from the credit saw steeper used-market drops. As a concrete example from the used market aggregator Plat Auto, a 2025 Equinox EV with only 1,600 miles was listed at $26,000, representing brutal depreciation. By contrast, a refreshed 2024 Model Y with 15,000 miles was listed at $35,000, which Brian called a strong deal.

3. The MKBHD-Tesla "beef" is overblown media hype. Marques Brownlee (MKBHD, 20.8 million YouTube subscribers) mentioned in his new Model Y Performance review that Tesla no longer proactively sends him vehicles, and he had to source the car himself from a local dealership. Brian explains this is simply because Tesla has no standing PR department — when they have a product to push, they push it; otherwise, there's no one making those calls. He notes Tesla had previously given Brownlee early Cybertruck access and that Brownlee called the Model S Plaid the best car of the decade, so there's no acrimony. Brownlee also sold his Cybertruck (he had an R1T already) and cancelled one of his two Roadster reservations, keeping one.

4. Uber's robotaxi strategy is more about platform than technology. The Uber CEO claimed Uber will handle more EV trips before the end of the decade than Tesla, and the company launched a new suite of services to let autonomous vehicle developers plug into its ride-hailing network. Brian is skeptical, noting Uber previously had its own AV project that it sold off. The actual pitch is essentially: "If you build a self-driving car, bring it to us." The realistic current partner is Waymo. Brian also corrects a common misconception, noting that Nvidia's Alamo (Drive) is a tool for building AV systems, not an autonomous driving system itself, contrary to speculation that legacy automakers like Ford could simply plug it in for FSD-equivalent capability.

5. Ford shut down its $5.8 billion Kentucky battery factory just months after opening, with 1,600 workers losing jobs. Brian frames this as partly a self-inflicted wound, noting Ford spent two decades telling customers EVs wouldn't work, then was surprised customers didn't buy their EVs. Ford's secretive next-generation EV pickup is rumored to feature 48V architecture, a structural battery pack, front and rear castings, and a small footprint similar to a Cybertruck — but has no release date and almost no public information. Brian is skeptical of Ford's broader EV commitment.

6. California's CARB voucher program for electric Class 8 semis is drawing competitor complaints. California earmarked $165 million in vouchers supporting Tesla Semi purchases, with the standard voucher worth $120,000 off the sticker price — enough to bring the Tesla Semi near price parity with diesel trucks on day one. Critics from competing EV manufacturers claim Tesla received preferential treatment and hasn't met certification standards. Brian dismisses this, noting the certification is about clean air compliance, which an all-electric vehicle automatically satisfies. He points out hydrogen trucks receive double the subsidy ($240,000+, up to $420,000 in pilot programs), which he notes was essentially Nikola's entire business model.

7. JD Power's 2026 EV Experience Ownership Study found 96% of current EV owners would consider another EV for their next vehicle. Brian attributes this largely to the fact that even mediocre EVs offer a fundamentally better drivetrain experience than equivalent gas vehicles, illustrating the point with his family's test drive of a Chevy Blazer EV — not a great car, but better than the gas Blazer it replaces.

8. Robo taxi accident statistics comparing AVs to human drivers are misleading. Brian has addressed this in multiple videos: the higher reported accident rate for robotaxis includes incidents that humans would never report, such as 1 mph parking lot contact with no damage, which AVs are legally required to log. It also includes not-at-fault incidents such as a bus striking a stationary vehicle.

Notable quotes and data:
- 96% of current EV owners would consider another EV (JD Power 2026 study)
- Tesla Semi CARB voucher: standard $120,000 off sticker; up to $420,000 in pilot/small business programs; hydrogen trucks get double the base subsidy
- 2025 Equinox EV with 1,600 miles listed used at $26,000; 2024 Model Y with 15,000 miles at $35,000
- MKBHD has 20.8 million YouTube subscribers
- Ford's shuttered Kentucky factory cost $5.8 billion and employed 1,600 workers
- Brian jokes that hitting 100,000 subscribers will earn viewers a "baby seal clubs me" moment
- On Tesla autopilot name change: "The problem with people whose job it is to connect dots... is a lot of times they run out of dots and start connecting things that are not even on the page."

2026-02-23.title: Why is Tesla Stock CRASHING Again?
2026-02-23.body:
Tesla stock dropped as much as 4.6% on this particular Monday, part of a broader market selloff driven by tariff uncertainty under Trump's latest announcements. The speaker, Joe (channel: jobhakdi), runs a Tesla-focused investment fund called Pioneer Alpha and uses the occasion to review fund performance, share his near-term caution and long-term bullishness on Tesla, and make several channel and fund announcements. His central thesis is that the current weakness is a temporary, fear-driven dislocation before a massive rally tied to Tesla's robotaxi deployment scaling in April 2025.

Key Points:

1. The selloff is tariff-driven noise, not a fundamental shift. The NASDAQ fell 1.13% and the Dow Jones fell 1.66% on the day. Tesla dropped up to 4.6% at its lowest point. Joe characterizes Trump's tariff announcements as "hot air" that the market is slowly learning to discount, noting the European Union and broader markets are also reacting with what he considers excessive fear.

2. Tesla has already absorbed significant downside from its peak. The stock fell roughly 25% from approximately $490 to under $400 since late 2024. Joe argues this was foreseeable — he claims to have warned viewers back in December about Q1 weakness and the "air gap" between the Robotaxi Day event and actual large-scale cybercab deployment. Because so much damage has already been done, he questions whether a dramatically worse leg down is likely.

3. He is short-term cautious but expects a rally once April catalysts materialize. Joe expresses concern about the remaining weeks of March, saying Tesla could dip below $400 again before recovering. However, he believes the key catalyst — cybercab deployment at hundreds of units, necessarily unsupervised because the vehicles have no steering wheels — will begin at meaningful scale around end of April. Once that happens, he predicts Tesla could reach $500 very quickly and potentially $600 shortly after, given the stock's historical pattern of sudden explosive moves after sideways consolidation.

4. The robotaxi tracker is showing incremental but real progress. At time of recording, the tracker showed 396 cybercabs with 8 unsupervised units, up from 7 the previous day. Joe acknowledges the tracker is imperfect — not all vehicles listed may be active, and the total fleet size could be larger or smaller than indicated — but defends it as the best available approximation of progress. He had predicted Tesla would hit 400 robotaxis that day and viewed the 396 figure as close confirmation.

5. Pioneer Alpha fund performance update. For the prior week, the fund was down only 0.17% versus Tesla down 1.35%, generating approximately 1.18% alpha. Year to date, the fund is down 2% while Tesla is down 8.43%, representing 6.31% alpha over Tesla. Joe notes that over a 6-month and 1-year horizon, Pioneer Alpha has dramatically outperformed even the NASDAQ, citing 84% cumulative alpha over the NASDAQ across longer timeframes. The month-to-date picture is hurt by a Robinhood position that fell 10% after earnings, though Joe says he does not regret that position.

6. Pioneer Alpha is now open to qualified investors internationally. Previously limited, the fund has expanded eligibility to Canada, the United Kingdom, the European Union, Switzerland, and the United States. Investors must complete a qualification process specific to their region. Joe clarifies the structure is bonds rather than an open fund, with a close expected before April. He emphasizes he cannot publicly discuss terms for non-US regions but can allow qualified investors to see them after completing eligibility verification at pioneeralpha.org.

7. Two new channel themes are being launched with distinct visual branding. "Pioneer Lands" (brownish/gold thumbnails) will cover building future civilization, technology, and society. A third theme in magenta will cover politics, including Germany, Europe, tariffs, Trump, and UK affairs. This segmentation is designed to help viewers self-select content and avoid frustration when investment content mixes with broader civilization or political topics.

8. The broader AGI context underpins his long-term bullishness. Joe argues we are in an age of AGI where productivity is surging and the "Magnificent 7" companies are seeing revenue and profitability climb. He dismisses talk of an AGI bubble, pointing out the market has been trying to sell off since November without success. He plans dedicated videos on what he calls the potentially serious societal consequences of AGI, while simultaneously arguing this is the force preventing a sustained market decline.

Conclusions and recommendations: Joe recommends staying positioned in Tesla with awareness that further short-term downside is possible through March. His core strategy involves an options "churn" approach designed to generate returns during sideways or range-bound price action, and a "leapro" (LEAPS options) strategy for capturing the eventual breakout. He stresses the importance of being pre-positioned before the cybercab catalyst arrives, given Tesla's history of violent, FOMO-driven rallies.

Notable quotes and data:
- Tesla drop from peak: "490 to under 400... we have dropped 25%"
- Year-to-date alpha: "We are up 6.31% over Tesla. Tesla is down 8.43% since January 1st. We are only down 2%."
- Long-term benchmark comparison: "84% alpha" over the NASDAQ across longer timeframes
- Price targets on a breakout: "We go to 500 in no time and probably to 600 in absolutely no time"
- On Tesla's price behavior: "Tesla is a stock that goes sideways and sideways forever... and when it happens, you add 50 or 100% in super short periods of time with total FOMO"
- Robotaxi tracker at time of recording: 396 total, 8 unsupervised units

2026-02-24.title: They’re Trying To Stop Tesla Now
2026-02-24.body:
This video covers Tesla's accelerating progress on the Cyber Cab robotaxi, competitive responses from Waymo and Uber, and other Tesla developments. Host Herbert and guest Joe Bacti, an economist and tech entrepreneur, discuss what these developments mean for Tesla investors.

Key Points:

1. Cyber Cab spotted on public roads in Palo Alto, California for the first time, with no steering wheel or side mirrors — previously sightings had been limited to Giga Texas.

2. Influencer Joe Techmire received a private tour of Giga Texas and confirmed hundreds of Cyber Cabs inside the factory, that production is already underway, and that the "unboxed" manufacturing method (five main structural components assembled separately) is being used.

3. The robotaxi tracker shows approximately 31 Cyber Cabs in deployment and 8 unsupervised units, with the count on a steep upward trajectory. Volume production is targeted for April 2026.

4. Joe Bacti argues that Cyber Cab production itself is not the bottleneck — the real question is whether Tesla's FSD software is ready for unsupervised deployment at scale. He outlines three investor scenarios: smooth April launch, dramatic delay followed by eventual deployment with a stock dip to around $200 before a major recovery, or a deeply depressing long-term delay.

5. Waymo submitted a proposal to California's DMV to ban Tesla from using the words "driverless," "self-driving," or "robo taxi" in marketing. This follows a December ruling that previously forced Tesla to drop "autopilot" and "full self-driving" terminology. Tesla simply changed its marketing language to comply and has now filed a lawsuit against the California DMV to reverse the original ruling.

6. A narrative claiming Tesla has four times more crashes than human drivers circulated widely, based on five January incidents. The hosts dispute this characterization, noting all five were minor fender-benders at under 4 mph, one involving a stationary Tesla struck by a bus.

7. Uber announced "Uber Autonomous Solutions," a comprehensive service suite for AV partners including training data collection via a Lucid vehicle fleet, data-enriched mapping, regulatory support, fleet financing, and remote operations. The hosts view this as a desperate strategy entirely dependent on Tesla failing, as Tesla robotaxi scaling would likely devastate Uber's business model.

8. Tesla is hiring Optimus motor and actuator design engineers in Athens, Greece, signaling continued Optimus robot development expansion.

9. The Cybertruck at a new $59,000 price point sold out through September within roughly one day of announcement, pushing new order delivery estimates out three to four months.
